VERDANA ANALYTICS — INTERNAL DOCUMENT
CLASSIFICATION: CONFIDENTIAL
DOCUMENT TYPE: MEETING TRANSCRIPT
DATE: MAY 1, 2025
SUBJECT: PRICING STRATEGY — FINAL DECISION MEETING

ATTENDEES:
- Priya Chandrasekaran (CEO)
- Marcus Okafor (CTO)
- Nadia Reis (Head of Product)
- Derek Huang (VP Sales)
- Tomoko Abe (Finance Lead)
- Jake Fernsby (Senior Sales Engineer) [remote, joined at 00:03:10]
- Lian Xu (Data Infrastructure Lead) [remote]
- Haruto Tanaka (Marketing Manager) [remote]

MEETING DURATION: 1h 42m
LOCATION: Conference Room B / Zoom Hybrid
RECORDING: Yes (internal archive only)
NOTETAKER: Haruto Tanaka
DIAL-IN: Zoom Meeting ID 8441-2290-5571

PRE-MEETING NOTE:
Tomoko distributed the pricing model workbook (v7, final) to all attendees at 8:47 AM via Slack #pricing-strategy. Derek shared the competitive survey summary PDF at 9:02 AM. Both documents are pinned in the channel for reference.

***

TRANSCRIPT — PRICING STRATEGY SESSION

[00:00:02] [Room audio check. Marcus confirms Zoom participants can hear. Lian Xu confirms from the Portland office. Haruto waves from his home office.]

[00:00:12] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: All right, let's get into this. We've been circling on pricing for three weeks and we need to lock it today. I don't want another "let's sleep on it" outcome — we walked out of two previous sessions without a decision and that's costing us real pipeline momentum. Tomoko, can you walk us through the final models?

[00:00:28] TOMOKO ABE: Sure. I've modeled three scenarios based on the competitive data Derek's team pulled and our cost structure. Let me share the spreadsheet. Everyone should see the "Pricing_v7_Final" tab — can you confirm on Zoom? Lian, Haruto, Jake, you see it?

[00:00:41] LIAN XU: Got it.

[00:00:42] HARUTO TANAKA: Yep, it's up.

[00:00:45] TOMOKO ABE: Great. So the core question is where we land relative to Rivalytics. They're at $49 per seat per month. That's their standard tier — they also have an enterprise tier at $89 but that's a different segment. For our purposes, $49 is the number. I've color-coded the three scenarios in the workbook: green for A, blue for B, orange for C.

Scenario A: $39/mo. This puts us 20% below Rivalytics. Pros: still premium-adjacent, healthy margins at 78%. Cons: might not be enough daylight to motivate switching. Derek, you had thoughts on this.

[00:01:15] DEREK HUANG: Yeah, $39 is the worst of both worlds. It's not cheap enough to be obviously better for price-sensitive buyers, and it's not premium enough to signal quality. I've talked to 23 prospects in the last month and the feedback is consistent — if we're going to compete on price, the gap needs to be meaningful. Like, "my CFO notices this" meaningful.

[00:01:33] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: Can you characterize those 23 prospects? Size, industry mix?

[00:01:37] DEREK HUANG: Sure. Roughly half are e-commerce, about a third are SaaS companies doing product analytics, and the rest are a mix — logistics, fintech, a couple of healthcare analytics shops. Headcount ranges from about 60 to 400 employees. These are solid mid-market companies with real analytics needs, not hobby projects. Most of them are currently on Rivalytics or one of the smaller competitors like Datapane or MetricStream.

[00:01:42] TOMOKO ABE: Scenario B: $29/mo. This is 41% below Rivalytics. Margins are 71%, which is still strong. The unit economics work — we hit break-even on a customer at month 4 instead of month 3, but lifetime value is actually higher because of the churn differential. I've got the full breakdown on rows 18 through 34 of the spreadsheet if anyone wants to follow along.

[00:02:03] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: Explain the churn piece.

[00:02:06] TOMOKO ABE: So I pulled Rivalytics's public data from their last investor presentation — they disclosed 15% annual SMB churn. That's actually not terrible for the segment, but it's high enough that their customer acquisition cost doesn't fully amortize for small accounts. Our thesis is that at $29, we pull in the price-sensitive mid-market accounts that are currently churning off Rivalytics because $49 feels like too much for what they're getting. These customers are inherently more price-sensitive, yes, but if we deliver value at $29, we should see churn below 10% because the value-to-price ratio is dramatically better.

[00:02:35] NADIA REIS: Tomoko, quick question on the churn model — are you assuming we match Rivalytics on feature parity at launch, or are you factoring in that we're missing some things like the dashboard builder?

[00:02:42] TOMOKO ABE: I'm factoring in our actual launch feature set. The churn model assumes that the accounts we acquire are API-first buyers who value real-time data over dashboards. The 10% churn target accounts for some percentage of customers who sign up expecting dashboard parity and leave when they don't find it. I've got that broken out as "feature gap churn" on row 28 — it's about 3 percentage points of the total.

[00:02:48] MARCUS OKAFOR: What about Scenario C?

[00:02:51] TOMOKO ABE: Scenario C is $19/mo. Aggressive undercut — 61% below Rivalytics. Margins drop to 58%. Break-even pushes to month 7. And honestly, I think this signals "cheap" in a way that hurts us with the accounts we actually want. Mid-market buyers are price-conscious but not bargain-hunters. $19 sounds like a tool you try for a week and forget about.

[00:03:04] HARUTO TANAKA: From a marketing perspective, I'd echo that. Positioning at $19 puts us in the same mental bucket as free-tier tools with usage caps. The blog content strategy we've been building assumes we're talking to decision-makers who have budget — they just want to spend it more efficiently. $19 undermines that narrative.

[00:03:10] [Jake Fernsby joins the Zoom call]

[00:03:14] JAKE FERNSBY: Sorry I'm late — customer call ran over. I caught the tail end of the $19 discussion. Can someone give me a thirty-second recap on where we are?

[00:03:19] DEREK HUANG: Three scenarios: $39, $29, $19. Tomoko walked through the financials. General consensus is forming around $29 — kills $19 on positioning, kills $39 on switching incentive. We're about to discuss that.

[00:03:18] NADIA REIS: I agree with Tomoko on $19. $19 also doesn't give us room to move. If we ever need to discount for annual contracts or run promotions, we're already at the floor. At $29 we can offer 20% annual discount and still be at $23, which is profitable. And frankly, annual discounts are table stakes in this market — every SaaS buyer's procurement team asks for one.

[00:03:28] MARCUS OKAFOR: Lian, do you have a perspective on the infrastructure cost side? At $19 our margins go to 58% — is that sustainable given what we know about scaling costs?

[00:03:34] LIAN XU: At $19 we'd need to be a lot more aggressive about compute optimization. Right now our streaming pipeline costs about $0.0034 per event processed. That's fine at current volume, but if we're acquiring accounts at a faster rate because of the lower price, and those accounts tend to send higher event volumes — because they chose us for real-time, so they'll actually use real-time — the per-account infrastructure cost goes up. At $29 we have comfortable headroom. At $19 it gets tight around the 150-account mark unless we rearchitect the buffering layer. I can share the cost projection doc after this meeting if people want the details.

[00:03:35] DEREK HUANG: I want to put some numbers behind the switching argument. I surveyed 14 current Rivalytics customers as part of our competitive research. Here's what I found:

Eight out of 14 said they'd consider switching if a comparable product was "significantly cheaper." When I pushed on what "significantly" meant, the consistent answer was "at least 30% less." $29 is 41% less — that clears the bar.

Six out of 14 said they're frustrated with Rivalytics's approach to data freshness. Their batch ETL process means dashboards update every 6-12 hours. For the companies doing real-time operations — logistics, e-commerce, support ops — that's a real pain point.

Three of those six are actively evaluating alternatives. Those are our beachhead accounts.

[00:04:05] JAKE FERNSBY: This tracks with what I'm seeing on the SE side. I ran a demo for two of those three accounts last week — a mid-size e-commerce company and a logistics outfit. Both of them were blown away by the latency difference. The logistics company, Routewell, they currently have a 9-hour lag on their shipment analytics. Their ops team literally makes routing decisions based on yesterday afternoon's data. When I showed them sub-second event streaming in our sandbox, the room went quiet. Their head of ops said, and I'm quoting, "This is what we thought Rivalytics was going to be."

[00:04:18] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: That's a great anecdote. Did they ask about pricing?

[00:04:20] JAKE FERNSBY: They did. I gave them the range we'd been discussing — "between $25 and $35 per seat" — and they didn't blink. They're currently paying $49 at Rivalytics for 18 seats, so they're at about $882 a month. Even at $35 they'd be saving over $250 a month. At $29 the savings are over $350. For a mid-market company running tight margins on logistics, that's noticeable.

[00:04:22] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: So the thesis is: $29 gets us past the price threshold for switching, and once they're evaluating, our real-time streaming architecture closes the deal. Price opens the door, product wins the room.

[00:04:35] DEREK HUANG: Exactly. And the mid-market segment is big enough to build a real business. We're talking about companies with 50-500 employees, typically 5-25 analytics seats. At $29/seat, a 15-seat account is $435/mo, $5,220/year. If we get 200 accounts in year one, that's over a million in ARR.

[00:04:48] HARUTO TANAKA: From a go-to-market perspective, the $29 number is also clean for messaging. "Under thirty bucks a seat" is an easy line for ads, for landing pages, for sales emails. $39 doesn't have that same simplicity. And the comparison to Rivalytics writes itself — "Same analytics, half the price, ten times faster." I already have three campaign concepts sketched around that value prop.

[00:04:55] NADIA REIS: Haruto, when you say "same analytics" — we need to be careful. We don't have dashboard parity. I don't want marketing writing checks that product can't cash.

[00:04:57] HARUTO TANAKA: Fair. I'll work with you on the exact language. We can say "real-time analytics" rather than "same analytics" — that's both accurate and differentiated.

[00:04:58] MARCUS OKAFOR: I'm fine with $29. My concern is whether we're anchoring ourselves. What happens if Rivalytics responds?

[00:05:08] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: They won't respond immediately. They have 2,000+ customers on the $49 plan. Dropping price means cannibalizing existing revenue. The institutional inertia alone buys us 6-9 months. By then we need to have enough market presence that we're competing on product, not just price.

[00:05:18] MARCUS OKAFOR: And what's our read on their engineering capacity to respond on the product side? If they decide to build real-time streaming to neutralize our advantage?

[00:05:22] LIAN XU: I can speak to that. I've looked at their tech stack — they've talked about it publicly at a couple of conferences, and some of their engineers post about it on their blog. They're running a classic batch architecture: scheduled ETL jobs pulling from source systems into a warehouse, then serving pre-computed aggregates to their dashboard layer. Moving to streaming would mean replacing the ingestion layer with something like Kafka or Flink, rewriting the serving layer to handle live queries, and rearchitecting the dashboard rendering to handle incremental updates instead of full refreshes. That's 12 to 18 months of serious engineering work for a team their size, and it would need to run in parallel with their existing product because they can't break service for 2,200 customers. It's not impossible, but it's a very heavy lift.

[00:05:27] TOMOKO ABE: I ran the sensitivity analysis on a Rivalytics price response. If they drop to $39, we're still 26% cheaper. If they drop to $29 to match, they're cutting their revenue per customer by 41% — that's a $4.2M annual hit on their current base assuming 80% of customers get the new rate. Their board won't approve that without existential pressure.

[00:05:40] DEREK HUANG: And if they try a tiered response — like keeping $49 for existing customers but offering $29 for new ones — that creates a two-tier customer base that's a nightmare for their sales team. Current customers find out and demand the lower rate. I've seen this play out at three different competitors in other markets. It always ends badly.

[00:05:48] TOMOKO ABE: I modeled that scenario too, actually. It's on tab 4 of the workbook, labeled "Rival Response — Tiered." The leakage rate from existing customers discovering the lower tier is historically about 40% within the first year, based on case studies from Zuora's pricing research. That turns their $4.2M hit into a $1.7M hit immediately, growing to $3.1M by month 12. Still very painful.

[00:05:52] DEREK HUANG: And even if they match on price, they can't match on architecture. Batch ETL vs streaming is a fundamental technology gap. You don't refactor that in a quarter.

[00:05:58] JAKE FERNSBY: Also worth noting — when I talk to prospects, the technical evaluation happens separately from the pricing conversation. The engineers doing the eval care about API quality, data freshness, and documentation. The procurement team cares about price. We win both conversations independently. Even if Rivalytics matched on price tomorrow, we'd still win the technical eval with every team that needs real-time data.

[00:06:05] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: All right. I'm calling it. We go with $29/mo per seat. Standard tier, no feature gating — everyone gets the full API. Enterprise tier we'll figure out in Q3 when we have actual enterprise prospects. Tomoko, lock the financial model. Derek, update the sales deck. Anyone disagree?

[00:06:15] MARCUS OKAFOR: No objection. I just want to flag that I'll need to do a capacity planning review to confirm we can support 200 accounts on the current infrastructure without a significant cost step-up. Lian and I can have that done by end of week.

[00:06:18] LIAN XU: Already on it. I've been running load projections in the staging environment. Preliminary numbers look good — we can handle 300 accounts on the current cluster before we need to add nodes. I'll have the full report by Friday.

[00:06:20] NADIA REIS: No objection from product. I'll document the $29 standard tier feature set and circulate it by tomorrow so everyone is working from the same definition.

[00:06:22] [No objections]

[00:06:24] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: Good. Let's move to the competitive positioning piece. Derek, you prepared a brief — walk us through it and then we'll discuss messaging.

[00:06:30] DEREK HUANG: Sure. I'll share the doc on screen. Everyone should have received this via email earlier today, but let me go through the highlights live.

***

COMPETITIVE INTELLIGENCE BRIEF
PREPARED BY: Derek Huang, VP Sales
DATE: May 1, 2025
CLASSIFICATION: INTERNAL ONLY

SUBJECT: RIVALYTICS MARKET POSITION AND VULNERABILITIES

METHODOLOGY NOTE:
This brief draws on the following sources:
- 23 prospect interviews conducted April 7-28, 2025
- 14 Rivalytics customer surveys (structured interviews, 30 min each)
- Rivalytics Q3 2024 investor update (publicly available via their blog)
- G2 review analysis (187 Rivalytics reviews, aggregated sentiment)
- Job posting analysis (LinkedIn, Lever, Greenhouse) for headcount and technology signals
- Conference presentations (DataOps Summit 2024, AnalyticsCon Q1 2025)

SUMMARY OF FINDINGS:

Rivalytics (founded 2019, Series C, ~$18M ARR based on public disclosures) is the incumbent in the SMB/mid-market analytics space. Key data points from our competitive research:

PRICING:
- Standard tier: $49/mo per seat
- Enterprise tier: $89/mo per seat (includes SSO, custom integrations, dedicated support)
- Annual discount: 15% (effectively $41.65/mo standard)
- No free tier; 14-day trial only
- Our position: $29/mo = 41% undercut on monthly, 30% undercut vs their annual rate
- Note: Rivalytics occasionally offers 20% first-year discounts for competitive win-backs, bringing effective rate to ~$39/mo. Even with this discount active, our $29 rate is 26% cheaper.

CUSTOMER BASE:
- Estimated 2,200 paying accounts (based on job postings mentioning "customer count" and G2 review velocity)
- Heavy concentration in e-commerce and marketing analytics
- Growing presence in product analytics for SaaS companies (newer segment for them)
- SMB churn: 15% annual (disclosed in Q3 2024 investor update)
- Enterprise churn: 6% annual (industry average for this segment)
- Net revenue retention: 108% (expansion within accounts offsets churn)
- Average account size estimated at 10-15 seats based on G2 review data (reviewers frequently mention team size)

PRODUCT WEAKNESSES:
1. Batch ETL architecture — data freshness is 6-12 hours. This is structural, not a bug. Their entire pipeline is built on scheduled batch jobs. Retrofitting real-time streaming would require a ground-up rewrite of their ingestion layer. Multiple G2 reviews cite this as the top frustration, particularly from operations-focused customers.
2. Dashboard-centric UX — Rivalytics's core value prop is "beautiful dashboards." But this creates lock-in to pre-built views. Power users want programmatic access. Their API is an afterthought — rate-limited, poorly documented, missing key endpoints. Their developer documentation was last updated October 2024 and still references deprecated v1 endpoints.
3. Pricing inflexibility — $49 is positioned for mid-market but is expensive for SMBs. Their 15% churn reflects this — smaller accounts join, get value, but eventually decide the cost isn't justified for their scale. Several G2 reviews explicitly mention "outgrowing the price" or "couldn't justify the cost for our team size."
4. Onboarding complexity — new customers report 2-3 week setup times due to connector configuration and dashboard templating. Our streamlined API-first approach targets sub-24-hour time-to-value.

PRODUCT STRENGTHS (honest assessment):
1. Dashboard builder is genuinely good — drag-and-drop, polished, non-technical users love it. G2 average rating for "ease of use" is 4.6/5. This is their moat and we should not pretend otherwise.
2. Brand recognition in the analytics space — they're the default recommendation on most "best analytics tools" listicles. They sponsor three major analytics conferences annually and have a strong content marketing presence.
3. Integration ecosystem — 40+ native connectors vs our current 12. Their connector library covers long-tail sources like Marketo, HubSpot, Intercom, and Zendesk that mid-market companies rely on.
4. Support quality — dedicated CSMs for accounts over $10K ARR. Their NPS for support is reportedly 62 (strong). Smaller accounts get email-only support with 24-hour response SLA.

VERDANA'S COMPETITIVE POSITION:
- Price: 41% cheaper (clear win)
- Real-time data: Streaming architecture (clear win)
- API-first: Programmatic access is a first-class citizen (clear win for technical buyers)
- Dashboards: We don't have one yet (clear loss — see product roadmap for Q2 plans)
- Integrations: 12 vs 40+ (gap, but we cover the top 8 that represent 85% of use cases: Snowflake, BigQuery, Postgres, MySQL, Redshift, S3, Stripe, Salesforce)
- Brand: Unknown vs established (we're fighting uphill on awareness)
- Documentation: Strong (our docs are API-first, auto-generated from OpenAPI spec, always current)
- Onboarding: Faster (API key → first query in under 10 minutes vs 2-3 weeks for Rivalytics)

BEACHHEAD ACCOUNTS (3 actively evaluating):
1. Routewell Logistics — 18 seats on Rivalytics, frustrated with 9-hour data lag for routing decisions. Demo completed, strong technical fit. Primary contact: VP Operations.
2. CartBridge (e-commerce) — 22 seats on Rivalytics, needs real-time inventory analytics for flash sales. Initial call completed, demo scheduled May 6. Primary contact: Head of Data.
3. [Name redacted pending NDA] — 14 seats, fintech vertical, evaluating multiple alternatives. Early stage, no demo yet. Primary contact through Derek.

TARGET SEGMENT PROFILE:
- Company size: 50-500 employees
- Analytics team size: 5-25 seats
- Industries: e-commerce, logistics, SaaS product analytics, fintech
- Key buying triggers: data freshness requirements, API access needs, cost optimization mandates
- Typical deal cycle: 3-6 weeks from first demo to signed contract

RECOMMENDED POSITIONING STATEMENT:
"Real-time analytics at half the price. Stop waiting 12 hours for yesterday's data."

ALTERNATIVE POSITIONING (for technical audiences):
"API-first analytics with sub-second streaming. Built for engineers who outgrew dashboards."

***

FINANCIAL MODEL — LOCKED
Prepared by: Tomoko Abe
Date: May 1, 2025
Version: 7 (Final)
Approved by: Priya Chandrasekaran

METHODOLOGY:
This model uses bottom-up unit economics for Year 1, validated against top-down TAM sizing. Customer acquisition assumptions are based on Derek's pipeline data and historical conversion rates from the beta period. Infrastructure costs are based on Lian Xu's capacity planning estimates as of April 28, 2025.

KEY ASSUMPTIONS:
- Price point: $29/mo per seat
- Average seats per account: 12
- Average MRR per account: $348
- Target accounts Year 1: 200
- Year 1 ARR target: $835,200
- Gross margin: 71%
- Customer acquisition cost (CAC): $1,400
- CAC payback period: 4.0 months
- Target annual churn: <10%
- Lifetime value (LTV) at 10% churn: $3,480
- LTV:CAC ratio: 2.49:1

COST STRUCTURE DETAIL:
| Category | Monthly Cost (at 200 accounts) | % of Revenue |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Cloud infrastructure (AWS) | $14,200 | 20.4% |
| Third-party data connectors | $2,100 | 3.0% |
| Support tooling (Intercom, Zendesk) | $890 | 1.3% |
| Monitoring & observability | $620 | 0.9% |
| Payment processing (Stripe) | $2,050 | 2.9% |
| Other variable costs | $340 | 0.5% |
| Total variable costs | $20,200 | 29.0% |
| Gross margin | — | 71.0% |

MONTHLY REVENUE RAMP (YEAR 1 PROJECTION):
| Month | New Accounts | Cumulative Accounts | MRR |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| May 2025 | 5 | 5 | $1,740 |
| Jun 2025 | 8 | 13 | $4,524 |
| Jul 2025 | 12 | 25 | $8,700 |
| Aug 2025 | 15 | 40 | $13,920 |
| Sep 2025 | 18 | 58 | $20,184 |
| Oct 2025 | 22 | 80 | $27,840 |
| Nov 2025 | 25 | 104 | $36,192 |
| Dec 2025 | 25 | 128 | $44,544 |
| Jan 2026 | 22 | 149 | $51,852 |
| Feb 2026 | 20 | 168 | $58,464 |
| Mar 2026 | 18 | 185 | $64,380 |
| Apr 2026 | 17 | 200 | $69,600 |

Note: Ramp assumes beachhead accounts close in May-June, word-of-mouth and content marketing drive acceleration through Q3-Q4, slight deceleration in Q1 2026 as early adopter segment saturates before broader market campaigns launch.

SENSITIVITY TO COMPETITIVE PRICING RESPONSE:
| Rivalytics Price | Our Discount vs Rival | Impact on Conversion | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $49 (current) | 41% | Baseline | Strong switching incentive |
| $39 (10% cut) | 26% | -15% conversion | Still meaningful gap |
| $29 (match) | 0% | -60% conversion | Compete on product only |
| $19 (undercut us) | -53% (we're premium) | -85% conversion | Existential — see risk section |

RISK ASSESSMENT:
Low probability that Rivalytics drops below $29 in the next 12 months. Their cost structure (larger team, office leases, Series C servicing) requires higher price points. Our lean structure ($890K annual burn) allows us to sustain $29 profitably where they cannot.

Additional risk factors considered:
- New entrant risk: Other startups could target the same price-performance segment. Mitigated by our streaming architecture moat and first-mover advantage in the "real-time + affordable" niche.
- Funding risk: If we don't hit the Year 1 ARR target, runway extends to 14 months at current burn. Sufficient to course-correct.
- Margin compression: If cloud costs increase (AWS price changes, higher-than-expected event volumes), margins could compress to 65%. Still viable but reduces LTV:CAC to 2.1:1.

***

POST-MEETING DISCUSSION (TRANSCRIPT CONTINUED)

[00:06:35] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: Tomoko, one more thing on the model. What's our break-even month on a company-wide basis, not per-customer?

[00:06:40] TOMOKO ABE: At the projected ramp, we cross cash-flow positive in month 9, so January 2026. That assumes we don't materially increase headcount before then. If we hire two more engineers in Q3 as planned, it pushes to month 11.

[00:06:48] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: That's fine. We have 16 months of runway at current burn. Even with the hires, we have margin.

[00:06:53] MARCUS OKAFOR: Lian, on the infrastructure side — are the cost numbers in Tomoko's model based on reserved instances or on-demand pricing?

[00:06:57] LIAN XU: On-demand, which is conservative. If we commit to 1-year reserved instances once we're confident in the traffic patterns, we can save about 30% on compute costs. That would push gross margin from 71% to roughly 75-76%. I've been holding off on reserved commitments because our usage patterns are still stabilizing from the beta period, but by August we should have enough data to commit.

[00:07:04] MARCUS OKAFOR: Good. Let's revisit that in the August infrastructure review.

[00:07:08] NADIA REIS: Last thing from me — on the "no feature gating" decision. I want to make sure everyone understands what that means concretely. Every customer at $29 gets: full API access with no rate limits beyond fair-use, all 12 connectors, streaming and batch ingestion, webhook notifications, and our query builder. The only thing we're holding back for the future enterprise tier is SSO, audit logs, and custom SLAs. Does that match everyone's understanding?

[00:07:20] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes. Full product, one tier, $29. Simple. Simple is how we win.

[00:07:24] DEREK HUANG: Works for sales. Simple pricing means shorter deal cycles. I don't have to negotiate tier configurations with procurement teams.

[00:07:28] JAKE FERNSBY: And from an SE perspective, it means every demo environment matches what the customer actually gets. No awkward "well, that feature is in the premium tier" moments during technical evals.

[00:07:33] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: All right. I think we're aligned. Let's get this locked and shipped. Haruto, make sure the meeting notes are distributed by end of day.

[00:07:38] HARUTO TANAKA: Will do. I'll post the notes in #pricing-strategy and email the full transcript to the distribution list.

[00:07:41] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: Thanks everyone. Good meeting.

[00:07:44] [Meeting ends. Zoom participants disconnect.]

***

ACTION ITEMS FROM PRICING MEETING:
1. [TOMOKO] Lock financial model with $29/mo, distribute to board — Due: May 2
2. [DEREK] Update all sales collateral with new pricing — Due: May 3
3. [NADIA] Confirm feature set for $29 standard tier (full API, no gating) — Due: May 2
4. [PRIYA] Brief investors on pricing rationale at next board check-in — Due: May 8
5. [MARCUS] Confirm infrastructure costs support 71% margin at projected scale — Due: May 3
6. [LIAN] Deliver full capacity planning report for 200-account scenario — Due: May 3
7. [HARUTO] Distribute meeting notes and transcript to all stakeholders — Due: May 1
8. [HARUTO] Draft three campaign concepts around $29 positioning for Nadia's review — Due: May 7
9. [JAKE] Schedule demo for CartBridge (beachhead account #2) — Due: May 6
10. [DEREK] Finalize positioning statement with Haruto, align on "real-time analytics" language — Due: May 5
11. [MARCUS + LIAN] August reserved instance review — Calendar hold sent

DECISION STATUS: LOCKED
APPROVED BY: Priya Chandrasekaran, CEO
DATE: May 1, 2025

***

SLACK THREAD — #pricing-strategy
May 1, 2025 — 2:14 PM

HARUTO TANAKA: Meeting notes and full transcript are now posted. PDF attached, also pinned in this channel. Key decision: $29/mo per seat, standard tier, no feature gating. Action items at the bottom — please check your name and confirm. @here

TOMOKO ABE: Confirmed. Financial model v7 is locked. Board distribution draft going out tonight for Priya's review before sending. One thing I want to flag — the $835K ARR target assumes we maintain a 12-seat average per account. If our early customers skew smaller (say, 8 seats), we'd need 250 accounts instead of 200 to hit the same number. I'll include a sensitivity table on seat count in the board package.

DEREK HUANG: Confirmed. Sales deck updates starting now. New pricing slides will be in the shared drive by EOD tomorrow. I'm also updating the objection-handling guide and the competitive battle card. Jake, can you review the technical positioning slides once they're done?

JAKE FERNSBY: Absolutely. Send them my way whenever they're ready. Also confirmed on my action items — CartBridge demo locked for May 6 at 10 AM PT. Their Head of Data and two engineers attending. I'm building a custom sandbox instance that mirrors their e-commerce data model so the demo feels tailored rather than generic.

NADIA REIS: Confirmed. Feature set doc circulating tomorrow AM. I'm going to be very explicit about what's included and what's enterprise-only (SSO, audit logs, custom SLAs) so there's zero ambiguity for sales.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

This document establishes Verdana Analytics's competitive positioning against Rivalytics, our primary competitor in the mid-market analytics space. It is intended for internal use by the sales team, marketing, and leadership. It should NOT be shared externally. Do not forward, screenshot, or summarize this document in any customer-facing communication. If a prospect asks directly about Rivalytics, use the battlecard language in Section 3 only.

The positioning is built on three pillars: architecture superiority (real-time streaming vs batch ETL), price advantage ($29 vs $49), and developer experience (API-first vs dashboard-first).

This document supersedes the informal competitive notes that Derek circulated in Slack on March 14. Those notes were based on incomplete information and should no longer be referenced. If you have printed copies, please destroy them.

SCOPE AND AUDIENCE

This competitive positioning document covers Rivalytics specifically. We are aware of several other players in adjacent spaces — Metabase (open-source, self-hosted), Amplitude (product analytics), Mixpanel (event tracking) — but Rivalytics is the only competitor we encounter consistently in head-to-head evaluations. In Q1, Rivalytics appeared in 11 of our 23 competitive deals. No other competitor appeared in more than 3. This document does not address those other players; separate one-pagers are available on the sales wiki for Metabase and Amplitude.

***

SECTION 1: WHY RIVALYTICS'S APPROACH IS WRONG

Rivalytics was founded in 2019 on a simple insight: mid-market companies need analytics but can't afford enterprise tools like Tableau or Looker. They built a dashboard-centric product — beautiful charts, drag-and-drop interface, non-technical users can build reports without writing SQL. This was the right product for 2019.

It is the wrong product for 2025. Here is why.

1.1 THE BATCH ETL PROBLEM

Rivalytics's architecture is built on scheduled batch ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) jobs. Data flows from customer sources (Stripe, Shopify, HubSpot, etc.) into Rivalytics's data warehouse via scheduled syncs that run every 6-12 hours. When a user opens a dashboard, they are looking at data that is 6-12 hours old.

To understand why this matters, it helps to be specific about what "batch ETL" means in their implementation. Based on what we've gathered from their public documentation, developer blog posts, and conversations with former Rivalytics customers, the pipeline works roughly as follows:

- EXTRACT: A scheduler (likely Airflow or a custom cron system) triggers connector jobs on a fixed schedule. Each connector authenticates with the source API, pulls new or modified records since the last sync, and writes raw JSON to a staging area. Rivalytics's documentation mentions sync intervals of "every 6 hours" for standard plans and "every 1 hour" for enterprise plans. In practice, customers report that sync jobs sometimes queue behind each other, meaning actual freshness can be worse than the stated interval.

- TRANSFORM: After extraction, a transformation layer applies schema mapping, deduplication, and business logic (currency conversion, timezone normalization, etc.). This runs sequentially after extraction completes, adding additional latency.

- LOAD: Transformed data is loaded into their query-optimized data warehouse (likely Snowflake or BigQuery based on their job postings). Dashboards query this warehouse layer.

The net result is that from the moment an event occurs in a customer's system (a Stripe charge, a Shopify order, a HubSpot deal stage change) to the moment it's visible in a Rivalytics dashboard, the minimum latency is the sync interval plus extraction time plus transformation time plus load time. For a 6-hour sync interval, this could mean 7-8 hours of total latency in practice. For the enterprise hourly sync, it's still 75-90 minutes.

This was acceptable when analytics meant "review last month's performance." It is not acceptable for modern use cases:

- E-commerce: A store running a flash sale needs to see revenue impact in real time, not tomorrow morning. If conversion rate drops because the checkout flow is broken, waiting 12 hours to see it in a dashboard means 12 hours of lost revenue. Marcus ran into this exact scenario at PeakCommerce (a prospect we won in April). Their ops team told us they once lost $14K in a single afternoon because a payment gateway misconfiguration wasn't caught until the next morning's Rivalytics refresh. They now get sub-second visibility through our streaming pipeline.

- Support operations: If ticket volume spikes because of a product bug, the support manager needs to see the correlation between deployment events and ticket creation in real time. A 12-hour-old dashboard shows yesterday's problems. One of our design partners, a SaaS company with a 22-person support team, told us their Rivalytics dashboards were functionally useless during incidents — by the time the data refreshed, the incident was already resolved or escalated through other channels.

- Marketing: Campaign performance data that's half a day old means you can't optimize ad spend during the campaign. You're always reacting to stale data. This is especially painful for companies running paid social campaigns where CPMs shift hourly and budget allocation decisions need to happen in real time.

Rivalytics cannot fix this with incremental improvements. Their entire data pipeline — ingestion, transformation, storage, and query layers — is designed around batch processing. Converting to real-time streaming would require rewriting the core architecture. Based on their team size (estimated 65 engineers from LinkedIn) and the scope of the rewrite, this is a 12-18 month project minimum. They would essentially be building a new product.

Rohan (our Head of Engineering) reviewed Rivalytics's public API documentation and open-source connector code (they open-source their connector framework under MIT license) and confirmed that the batch assumptions are deeply embedded. Their connector interface defines a `sync()` method that returns a full dataset snapshot — there's no incremental change stream or webhook handler. Retrofitting streaming onto this interface would break every existing connector.

NOTE FOR SALES: Do not claim that Rivalytics's data is "always wrong" or "unreliable." Their data is accurate — it's just stale. The distinction matters because sophisticated buyers will call us out if we overstate the case. The accurate framing is: "Their data is correct as of the last sync. The question is whether 6-12 hour old data is sufficient for your use case."

1.2 THE DASHBOARD TRAP

Rivalytics's moat is their dashboard builder. It's genuinely good — polished UI, extensive widget library, smooth data binding. Non-technical users love it. Credit where it's due: their drag-and-drop chart builder is probably the best in the mid-market. Jess looked at their most recent Product Hunt launch page (February 2025) and the UI screenshots are impressive. They've clearly invested heavily in the presentation layer.

But the dashboard-first approach has a structural limitation: it locks users into pre-built views. Every question a user can answer must be anticipated in advance by someone who builds a dashboard. If a VP of Sales wants to see "deals closed by region where the sales cycle exceeded 45 days and the deal size was above $50K" — and nobody built that specific dashboard — the VP is stuck. They either file a request with their data team and wait, or they don't get the answer.

We've heard this frustration directly from prospects. In Derek's Q1 prospect interviews, 7 of 18 respondents who were evaluating or using Rivalytics mentioned some version of "I can see what someone decided I should see, but I can't explore on my own." The exact phrasing varied, but the underlying complaint is the same: the dashboard model assumes you know what questions you want to ask before you need the answers.

Rivalytics has tried to address this with a "Custom Explore" feature (launched November 2024 per their changelog). It allows users to select dimensions and metrics from dropdown menus and generate ad-hoc charts. But it's constrained to pre-defined dimensions and metrics — users can't construct arbitrary queries, join across data sources, or apply custom calculations. It's a more flexible dashboard builder, not a query engine.

Verdana's API-first approach inverts this. Any question that can be expressed as a query can be answered instantly. The API doesn't constrain what you can ask — it exposes all the data and lets the user (or their tools) construct the query. This is strictly more powerful.

To illustrate concretely: with Verdana's API, a data engineer can write a Python script that pulls real-time Stripe revenue data, joins it with HubSpot deal stage data, applies a custom weighting formula, and pushes the result to a Slack channel every 15 minutes. With Rivalytics, this workflow is impossible — there's no real-time data access, the API doesn't support cross-source joins, and the query capabilities are limited to what the dashboard builder exposes.

The tradeoff is accessibility: non-technical users can't write API queries. This is why we're building a dashboard for Q2. But our dashboard will be a layer ON TOP of the API, not a replacement for it. Users who want drag-and-drop get it. Users who want programmatic access get that too. Rivalytics's architecture doesn't allow this — their API is an afterthought, bolted on after the dashboard was built.

Nadia wants to emphasize: our Q2 dashboard is not a "me too" feature. It's architecturally different from Rivalytics's dashboard because it queries our real-time streaming layer directly. A Verdana dashboard showing "revenue today" updates every second. A Rivalytics dashboard showing "revenue today" shows revenue as of the last batch sync, which could be this morning. The visual presentation might look similar, but the underlying data freshness is fundamentally different.

TIMELINE NOTE: The Q2 dashboard is on track. Nadia confirmed that the frontend team (Lin, Amir, and contract designer Sarah K.) have completed wireframes and are in sprint 3 of 6. Target ship date is July 14. Beta access for select customers starts June 23. If a prospect asks "when do you have dashboards?", the answer is "July, with beta access available in late June."

1.3 THE INTEGRATION GAP (AND WHY IT DOESN'T MATTER AS MUCH AS IT LOOKS)

Rivalytics has 40+ native data source connectors. We have 14 (at launch). This looks like a significant gap. It is not, for two reasons.

First, our 14 connectors cover the data sources used by 85% of our target market (based on Derek's prospect survey of 42 companies). Here's the breakdown of what those 42 companies actually use:

| Data Source | % of Surveyed Companies Using | Verdana Connector? |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Stripe | 78% | Yes |
| Shopify | 52% | Yes |
| HubSpot | 64% | Yes |
| Salesforce | 48% | Yes |
| Google Analytics | 71% | Yes |
| PostgreSQL (direct) | 62% | Yes |
| MySQL (direct) | 38% | Yes |
| Segment | 31% | Yes |
| Slack | 29% | Yes |
| Zendesk | 26% | Yes |
| Intercom | 21% | Yes |
| Jira | 24% | Yes |
| GitHub | 19% | Yes |
| BigQuery (direct) | 17% | Yes |
| QuickBooks | 14% | No |
| Asana | 12% | No |
| Notion | 10% | No |
| Netsuite | 7% | No |
| Zuora | 5% | No |

The long tail of Rivalytics's 40+ connectors includes niche tools that serve less than 2% of their customer base each. We're talking about connectors like ChargeBee, Recurly, Pendo, Gainsight, and similar — tools used by specific segments but not broadly adopted across the mid-market.

Second, our API-first architecture means users can build custom connectors with a few lines of code. Rivalytics's users can't do this because the system is dashboard-centric — there's no programmatic data ingestion path. Our connector gap is a documentation problem, not an architecture problem.

To be specific about what "a few lines of code" means: our ingest API accepts JSON payloads via HTTP POST. A custom connector is literally a script that reads data from a source and POSTs it to our endpoint. Marcus built a working QuickBooks connector during a sales demo last month — it took 47 lines of Python and about 20 minutes, including auth setup. We've published this as an example in our docs.

For sales purposes: if a prospect mentions a data source we don't natively support, don't treat it as a loss. Ask them if they have a developer or data engineer on the team. If yes, the custom connector path is a strength, not a weakness — it demonstrates the flexibility of our architecture. If no (purely non-technical team), then yes, this is a gap, and you should be honest about it.

CONNECTOR ROADMAP (for internal reference only — do not share timelines with prospects):

- Asana: July 2025
- Notion: August 2025
- NetSuite: TBD (complex API, enterprise-oriented — deprioritized)

| Dimension | Verdana Analytics | Rivalytics | Advantage |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Price (standard) | $29/mo per seat | $49/mo per seat | Verdana (+41%) |
| Data freshness | Real-time (<200ms) | 6-12 hours (batch ETL) | Verdana |
| Architecture | Streaming-first | Batch ETL | Verdana |
| API quality | First-class, full access | Afterthought, rate-limited | Verdana |
| Dashboard builder | Not at launch (Q2) | Polished, mature | Rivalytics |
| Native connectors | 14 | 40+ | Rivalytics |
| Brand recognition | New entrant | Established (est. 2019) | Rivalytics |
| Support | Email + docs | Dedicated CSM (>$10K ARR) | Rivalytics |
| SSO/Enterprise | Not at launch (Q3) | Available ($89/mo tier) | Rivalytics |
| Developer experience | Excellent (SDKs, docs) | Basic | Verdana |

NOTES ON THE MATRIX:

- Price: Rivalytics's $49/mo is for their "Professional" plan. They also have a free tier (limited to 3 data sources, 1 dashboard, 1 user) and an enterprise tier at $89/mo per seat (includes SSO, audit logs, dedicated CSM, priority support). Our $29/mo is flat — no feature gating, no tiered access. Every paying customer gets full API access, all 14 connectors, unlimited dashboards (when available), and unlimited data retention. Derek's analysis of 8 competitive deals in Q1 shows that the price delta is the deciding factor in roughly 30% of wins.

- Data freshness: Our sub-200ms claim is measured as end-to-end latency from event occurrence to API query availability, tested under load with our standard connector set. This is documented in our public benchmarks page (verdana.dev/benchmarks). Rivalytics does not publish comparable latency numbers. Their documentation says "data refreshes every 6 hours" for standard plans, and their enterprise page mentions "hourly syncs" but doesn't define latency precisely.

- API quality: Rivalytics's API is documented at api.rivalytics.com/docs. Marcus reviewed it thoroughly and noted the following limitations: rate limited to 100 requests/minute (vs our 1000/minute), no streaming endpoints, no webhook support, no batch query endpoint, and several dashboard-only features (alerts, scheduled reports) that aren't exposed via API at all. Their API feels like it was built to satisfy a checkbox requirement rather than as a primary interface.

- Dashboard builder: We should be honest about this gap internally even if we don't lead with it externally. Rivalytics's dashboard builder is genuinely best-in-class for the mid-market. They've had 6 years to iterate on it. Our Q2 dashboard will be functional and competitive, but it won't match Rivalytics's polish on day one. Nadia estimates we'll reach feature parity by Q4 2025 and surpass them in Q1 2026 (because our dashboards will have real-time data, which theirs structurally cannot).

- Support: Rivalytics offers dedicated Customer Success Managers for accounts above $10K ARR. We currently offer email support with a target 4-hour response time during business hours (Pacific time). We don't have the headcount for dedicated CSMs yet. This is a real gap for enterprise-adjacent buyers. Priya has approved hiring 2 CSMs in Q3, which will help, but until then we need to be upfront about this.

WIN SCENARIOS (where we win deals):
1. Technical buyer (data engineer, analytics lead) who values API access and real-time data
2. Price-sensitive mid-market company ($50-500 employees) where $29 vs $49 is a meaningful difference
3. Companies with real-time use cases (e-commerce, support ops, logistics)
4. Teams that want to build custom integrations and workflows on top of analytics data

EXPANDED WIN SCENARIO DETAIL:

Scenario 1 is our strongest. In Q1, 8 of our 11 wins against Rivalytics had a technical buyer as the primary decision-maker or key influencer. These are people who immediately understand the value of an API-first architecture and real-time streaming. They don't need to be convinced — they need to be shown the docs and given a sandbox. Marcus's recommended demo flow for technical buyers: (a) show the API explorer, (b) run a live query against the demo dataset, (c) show the Python SDK with a 10-line integration example, (d) show the real-time event stream.

Scenario 2 is context-dependent. The $20/seat/month difference matters a lot at 10-50 seats ($200-$1000/month savings) and matters less at 3-5 seats ($60-$100/month). For small teams, we need another differentiator beyond price. Derek reviewed our Q1 pipeline and found that in deals with 20+ seats, we won on price alone in 3 out of 4 competitive evaluations. In deals with fewer than 10 seats, price was mentioned as a factor but was never the sole deciding criterion.

Scenario 3 applies when the buyer's team has strong real-time operational needs — think logistics companies tracking delivery status, or SaaS platforms monitoring feature adoption during a rollout. These prospects immediately grasp the streaming advantage because their daily workflows are hampered by batch-delayed data.

Scenario 4 tends to correlate with companies that already use other tools in Rivalytics's ecosystem (their Slack integration, their shared dashboard links for board presentations). Switching costs are higher when the analytics tool is embedded in cross-functional workflows.

LOSS SCENARIOS (where Rivalytics wins):
1. Non-technical buyer who needs dashboards immediately and won't wait for Q2
2. Enterprise buyer who needs SSO, audit logs, and dedicated support now
3. Company that needs niche connectors we don't support (and can't build their own)
4. Buyer who values brand recognition and established track record over price/tech

EXPANDED LOSS SCENARIO DETAIL:

Scenario 1 is our most common loss. In Q1, 5 of our 7 losses to Rivalytics were to non-technical buyers (typically a VP of Marketing or Head of Operations) who needed dashboards on day one. These prospects often said something like "your tech sounds great, but I need charts in front of my CEO next week." The Q2 dashboard will close this gap, but until July we will continue losing these deals. Derek's interim mitigation: offer these prospects a 90-day free trial that extends through our dashboard launch. This has converted 2 of 5 losses into "pending" opportunities.

Scenario 4 is harder to counter. Some buyers, especially at companies with 200+ employees, default to the "safe" choice. Rivalytics has case studies, G2 badges, a Gartner mention, and 6 years of operating history. We have none of that. This will improve with time but cannot be rushed. Jess is working on our first 3 case studies (PeakCommerce, DataSync Labs, and Meridian Logistics) targeted for publication in June.

SECTION 3: SALES BATTLECARD

This section is designed to be used in real-time during sales conversations. Print it out, keep it on your desk, or bookmark it. The language below has been tested in live calls by Derek and Marcus.

WHEN A PROSPECT MENTIONS RIVALYTICS:

Opening: "Rivalytics is a good product for teams that want pre-built dashboards. If your primary need is drag-and-drop charts, they're solid. But if you need real-time data, programmatic access, or you're cost-conscious, there are important differences."

NOTE: This opening deliberately acknowledges Rivalytics's strength. Do not start by attacking them. Sophisticated buyers respect honesty and distrust vendors who trash-talk competitors. Lead with acknowledgment, then pivot to differentiation. Marcus tested several opening variations in Q1 calls and found that starting with a genuine compliment about Rivalytics's dashboards increased prospect engagement and extended average call duration by about 4 minutes. The worst-performing opener was "Rivalytics is outdated" — two prospects ended the call early after that framing.

OBJECTION: "Rivalytics has dashboards, you don't."
RESPONSE: "You're right — we're launching API-first because our earliest customers are technical teams who want programmatic access. Our dashboard is coming in July. But here's the key question: do you need a dashboard on day one, or do you need accurate, real-time data on day one? Because Rivalytics's dashboards show data that's 6-12 hours old."

FOLLOW-UP (if they press): "I'm happy to set up a 90-day trial so you can start integrating your data sources now and have dashboards ready when they launch. Your data pipeline will be producing real-time insights from day one — the dashboard is just the visualization layer on top."

OBJECTION: "Rivalytics has more integrations."
RESPONSE: "They do — 40+ vs our 14. But let me ask: which data sources do you actually use? [Show the list of 14]. We cover the top data sources that 85% of mid-market companies use. And because we're API-first, your team can build a custom connector in an afternoon if you need one that's not on our list."

FOLLOW-UP (if they need a specific missing connector): "Which connector do you need? Let me check our roadmap — we're adding new connectors every month. And in the meantime, I can show you how our ingest API works — it takes about 20 minutes to build a custom connector. Marcus actually built a QuickBooks connector live during a demo last month."

OBJECTION: "$29 seems too cheap — what's the catch?"
RESPONSE: "No catch. We're a 14-person team with lean infrastructure. Rivalytics has 65+ employees, office leases, and Series C investors to service. Their cost structure requires $49. Ours doesn't. You're not getting less product — you're getting a company with better unit economics."

FOLLOW-UP (if they express concern about our size/viability): "Fair concern. We're venture-backed with 18 months of runway, we're growing revenue 22% month-over-month, and we have 47 paying customers including [name 2-3 recognizable logos if permitted]. We're small by choice — it keeps us fast and keeps your price low."

OBJECTION: "We're already using Rivalytics. Why would we switch?"
RESPONSE: "I'm not going to tell you to rip out a working system. But let me ask: are there use cases where 6-12 hour old data is costing you? If you're running real-time operations — flash sales, incident response, campaign optimization — you might benefit from running Verdana alongside Rivalytics for those specific use cases. We have several customers who started that way and gradually migrated over."

NOTE: This "land and expand" approach works well with existing Rivalytics customers. Don't push for full replacement — push for a parallel deployment on a specific real-time use case. Once they see the value of fresh data, the migration conversation happens naturally. Marcus has used this approach successfully in 3 deals this quarter. The typical pattern: the prospect starts with Verdana on a single real-time use case (usually e-commerce monitoring or support ticket correlation), runs both tools in parallel for 30-60 days, and then asks for a broader rollout. Two of those three accounts have since expanded to full-org deployments.

- Sub-200ms query latency on streaming data (vs 6-12 hour staleness)
- 14 native connectors covering 85% of target market data sources
- Python and JavaScript SDKs on PyPI and npm
- $29/mo per seat — no hidden fees, no feature gating, full API access
- Streaming architecture built from day one (not retrofitted)

- 47 paying customers as of May 1, 2025
- 99.7% uptime over the last 90 days (status page: status.verdana.dev)
- Average integration time: 2.3 hours from signup to first query (based on onboarding analytics)
- NPS of 62 (surveyed April 2025, n=31)

DO NOT SAY (things that will get us in trouble):
- Do not claim Rivalytics's data is "wrong" or "inaccurate" — it's stale, not wrong
- Do not share specific Rivalytics customer names or claim they're switching to us
- Do not promise specific feature ship dates to prospects — say "Q2" or "Q3," not "July 14"
- Do not disparage Rivalytics's team, investors, or business model
- Do not share this document or any part of it with prospects, partners, or press

***

SECTION 4: COMPETITIVE MONITORING

We are actively monitoring Rivalytics for:
1. Pricing changes — any modification to their $49 standard or $89 enterprise tier
2. Product releases — particularly any real-time or streaming features
3. Hiring patterns — job postings mentioning "streaming," "Kafka," "Flink" would signal architecture investment
4. Customer sentiment — G2 reviews, Twitter/LinkedIn complaints, support forum posts
5. Fundraising — any new round would give them runway for aggressive moves

MONITORING METHODS:

- Derek has set up Google Alerts for "Rivalytics" and "Rivalytics pricing"
- Jess monitors their blog (rivalytics.com/blog), changelog (rivalytics.com/changelog), and social media accounts weekly
- Marcus reviews their API documentation monthly for new endpoints or capability changes
- Rohan checks their open-source connector repo (github.com/rivalytics/connectors) for commits that might signal architectural direction
- Derek has informal relationships with 3 former Rivalytics customers who occasionally share insights (all under NDA)

CURRENT ASSESSMENT (May 2025):
- No signs of pricing changes
- No job postings related to streaming/real-time architecture
- Recent G2 reviews (last 30 days): 3 of 7 mention "stale data" or "data freshness" as a negative
- No fundraising signals
- Their most recent blog post (April 29) announced a Notion connector and improvements to their chart color palette — incremental, not strategic
- Their most recent changelog entry (May 1) lists bug fixes and a new CSV export option for dashboards — no architectural changes
- RISK LEVEL: LOW for next 6 months

LONGER-TERM RISK ASSESSMENT:

The main strategic risk is that Rivalytics decides to build real-time capabilities. As noted in Section 1.1, this would be a 12-18 month rewrite. If they started today (and we have no evidence they have), the earliest they could ship a competitive real-time product is Q3 2026. By that time, we expect to have 200+ customers, a mature dashboard, SSO/enterprise features, and 25+ connectors. The window for us to establish market position is now through Q1 2026.

A secondary risk is that Rivalytics cuts pricing aggressively to match or undercut our $29. Their cost structure (65+ employees, Series C obligations) makes this difficult to sustain, but they could do a promotional pricing campaign for 6-12 months if they decide winning market share is worth the margin compression. Derek is monitoring for any pricing signals.

A third, lower-probability risk is acquisition. If Rivalytics is acquired by a larger platform (Salesforce, HubSpot, Datadog), the combined entity could bundle analytics for free or near-free as part of a larger platform play. This would undercut our price advantage entirely. We have no intelligence suggesting this is imminent, but it's worth noting as a scenario. If it happens, our response would need to shift from price competition to standalone-product-quality competition.

TRANSCRIPT — LAUNCH GO/NO-GO MEETING

[00:00:00] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: All right, let me get the Zoom participants loaded. Marcus, can you confirm the remote folks are on?

[00:00:06] MARCUS OKAFOR: Yeah, I see Rachel, Victor, Claudia, and Sam on the call. Audio checks good?

[00:00:10] RACHEL DOMINGUEZ: Good on my end.

[00:00:11] VICTOR SATO: Same here.

[00:00:12] SAM WHITFIELD: Yep.

[00:00:15] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: This is the go/no-go meeting. We launch on May 15 or we don't. Let's go around the room. Each owner gives a status — green, yellow, or red. If anything is red, we discuss. If everything is green or yellow-with-mitigation, we ship. Marcus, start us off.

[00:00:35] MARCUS OKAFOR: Infrastructure: GREEN. We're running on AWS us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Load testing shows we can handle 500 concurrent accounts with current provisioning. Auto-scaling is configured. Database is on RDS with read replicas. The streaming pipeline is stable — we've been running it internally for three weeks with no incidents. Monitoring is live in Datadog. On-call rotation starts May 14.

[00:00:58] MARCUS OKAFOR: Victor, do you want to add anything on the ops side?

[00:01:01] VICTOR SATO: Sure. We've got Terraform managing all infrastructure, so nothing is manually provisioned. The us-east-1 cluster is running three t3.xlarge app servers behind an ALB, with auto-scaling configured to add instances at 70% CPU. The eu-west-1 region mirrors that setup. RDS is a db.r6g.large primary in us-east-1 with two read replicas, and a cross-region read replica in eu-west-1 for the European connector traffic.

[00:01:22] VICTOR SATO: On monitoring — we've got 47 Datadog monitors configured. The critical ones are API latency (p99 threshold at 800ms, currently sitting at 340ms), error rate (threshold 1%, currently 0.02%), and database connection pool utilization (threshold 80%, currently at 22%). PagerDuty is wired up. The on-call schedule starts May 14 — Marcus has first rotation, I have second, Amir has third. Eight-hour shifts.

[00:01:48] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: And we tested failover?

[00:01:50] VICTOR SATO: Yes. We did a controlled failover drill last Tuesday. Simulated an us-east-1 AZ outage. The ALB routed around the downed instance in 38 seconds. No dropped requests during the failover. RDS automatic failover to the standby took about 90 seconds, which is within AWS's documented window.

[00:02:02] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: That's solid. Ninety seconds of database downtime — is that acceptable for launch?

[00:02:06] MARCUS OKAFOR: For an AZ failure, yes. That's a rare event and 90 seconds is industry standard for managed RDS failover. If we were multi-region active-active, we could do better, but that's a different cost profile. For launch traffic, this is more than adequate.

[00:02:16] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: Understood. Good. Amir?

[00:02:18] AMIR PATEL: Core API: GREEN. All endpoints are tested. Rate limiting is in place — 100 requests per minute for standard tier, we can adjust. The 14 data connectors are all passing integration tests. Intercom and Zendesk were the last two — finished yesterday. Python SDK is on PyPI version 0.9.1, JavaScript SDK is on npm version 0.9.0. API documentation is published at docs.verdana-analytics.io.

[00:02:46] AMIR PATEL: Let me run through the connectors quickly for the record, since this has been the long pole. The 14 native connectors at launch are: Salesforce, HubSpot, Stripe, Intercom, Zendesk, PostgreSQL, MySQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Google Analytics, Mixpanel, Segment, Amplitude, and Shopify. Each one has a dedicated integration test suite — we're running those in CI on every merge to main. Total of 312 integration test cases across all 14 connectors, all passing as of this morning's build.

[00:03:12] RACHEL DOMINGUEZ: I can confirm that. I ran the full regression suite at 6am today. 312 integration tests, 847 unit tests, all green. The only flaky test we had — the Snowflake timeout issue — was fixed on Wednesday when Amir increased the connection timeout from 10 to 30 seconds. It's been stable since.

[00:03:28] AMIR PATEL: Thanks, Rachel. Batch export endpoint — this was the last piece — is done and tested. Users can export query results as CSV or JSON with up to 100K rows per export. Streaming queries support real-time subscriptions via WebSocket.

[00:03:42] AMIR PATEL: The WebSocket implementation is running on a dedicated set of servers — separate from the REST API servers — so a spike in streaming connections won't affect API latency. We tested with 200 simultaneous WebSocket connections and saw stable memory usage around 1.2GB per instance. Each connection gets its own event loop, and we're using Redis pub/sub as the message broker between the query engine and the WebSocket servers.

[00:04:01] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: What happens if someone tries to export more than 100K rows?

[00:04:04] AMIR PATEL: They get a 413 response with a clear error message explaining the limit and suggesting they use the streaming API for larger datasets. Claudia wrote a help doc on that — it's in the docs portal under "Data Export Limits."

[00:04:14] CLAUDIA FERREIRA: Correct. There's also a code example showing how to paginate through large datasets using the streaming endpoint. Python and JavaScript versions.

[00:04:21] AMIR PATEL: One yellow flag: the webhook delivery system occasionally drops events under high load. Amir ran a stress test with 10,000 webhooks per minute and saw a 2% drop rate. For launch traffic this won't be an issue — we expect maybe 100 webhooks per minute total — but it's something to fix in the next sprint.

[00:04:39] MARCUS OKAFOR: Just to clarify on the webhook architecture — we're using an SQS queue for webhook delivery, and the 2% drop is happening at the consumer level when the target endpoints are slow to respond. The events aren't lost; they're in the dead letter queue. So it's really a retry issue, not a data loss issue. We can add exponential backoff retry logic in the post-launch sprint and bring that drop rate to near zero.

[00:04:58] AMIR PATEL: That's right. The DLQ retains failed messages for 7 days. So even in the worst case, we have a window to replay them.

[00:05:04] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: That's fine for launch. Flag it for the post-launch sprint. Jess?

[00:05:08] JESS THORNTON: User management and RBAC: GREEN. Three roles — admin, editor, viewer. Invite flow works. Password reset works. Session management works. I tested with 50 concurrent users across 5 accounts — no issues.

[00:05:22] JESS THORNTON: Let me walk through the permissions matrix briefly. Admins can do everything — manage billing, invite users, create and revoke API keys, configure connectors, and obviously run queries. Editors can run queries, configure connectors, and create their own API keys, but they can't manage billing or invite users. Viewers are read-only — they can run queries against existing connectors but can't modify anything.

[00:05:45] JESS THORNTON: Authentication is handled via JWT tokens with a 1-hour expiry and refresh token rotation. We're using bcrypt for password hashing with a cost factor of 12. Rate limiting on the login endpoint is tighter than the general API — 10 attempts per minute per IP, with a 15-minute lockout after 5 failed attempts on a single account.

[00:06:02] NADIA REIS: What about SSO? I know we said it's not launch scope, but have we gotten questions from the pipeline?

[00:06:07] DEREK HUANG: Two of my warm prospects asked about SAML SSO. I told them it's on the Q3 roadmap. Neither one made it a dealbreaker, but it's worth noting.

[00:06:14] JESS THORNTON: The auth layer is designed to plug in SSO — I've got the interface abstracted. When we get to it, it's probably a 2-3 week implementation, not a rewrite.

[00:06:22] JESS THORNTON: One note on the dashboard: I've been maintaining the grid system and chart rendering code in a feature branch. It's not shipping but it's not bitrotting either. When we start the Q2 dashboard sprint, I can hit the ground running. Estimated 4-5 weeks to shippable from where we are now.

[00:06:38] JESS THORNTON: The branch is called `feature/dashboard-builder-v2`. Last rebased on main yesterday. What's missing is the data binding layer, saved dashboard persistence, and sharing/embedding. Those are the 4-5 weeks.

I also did manual exploratory testing on the invite flow — tried edge cases like inviting an email that's already in the system, inviting with a role that doesn't exist, inviting to a full account. It works, the information hierarchy is correct, and the code samples render properly, but the typography doesn't match our brand guidelines and the color palette is the ReadMe default blue instead of our green. It's a cosmetic issue — no functional impact — but it'll bother me every time I look at it. I've got the design spec ready; it's about 2-3 days of front-end work to re-skin it.

[00:14:02] LINA CHOI: The onboarding flow I'm more proud of. It's a 4-step wizard: create account, connect first data source, run a sample query, and view results. Average completion time in our internal testing was 6 minutes and 40 seconds. The connector step has inline help that adapts to which connector the user selects — so if they pick Salesforce, they see Salesforce-specific instructions with screenshots of where to find the API credentials in Salesforce Settings.

[00:14:28] LINA CHOI: I also want to flag again — I know I'm a broken record — that we will hear from users about the dashboard. I've prepared a response template for the support team: "We hear you. The dashboard builder is our top priority for Q2 and we're targeting a July release. In the meantime, here's how to build visualizations using our API with [Grafana / Retool / custom code]." That should buy us some goodwill.

[00:14:52] LINA CHOI: I also put together a one-page "Visualization Quick Start" guide that shows how to connect Grafana to our API in under 5 minutes. It's linked from the response template. Claudia reviewed it for accuracy.
