CANDIDATE: JAMES PARK (Toast)
ON-SITE DATE: June 4
INTERVIEWERS: Marcus (system design), Amir (technical deep-dive), Nadia (product collaboration), Priya (culture)
YEARS OF EXPERIENCE: 4
CURRENT TITLE: Software Engineer II, Toast (Payments Infrastructure team)

TAKE-HOME REVIEW:
- Score: 6/10
- Reviewers: Marcus (primary), Jess (secondary)
- Functional but not exceptional. REST API worked correctly. PostgreSQL queries were correct but not optimized. No streaming component — he used polling. Documentation was minimal. The code was organized into a single module with inline configuration. Tests covered the happy path but didn't test error cases, edge cases, or concurrent access. Jess noted that the polling approach, while functional, suggests he may not have production experience with event-driven architectures.
- On the positive side: the code was readable, variable names were clear, and the API design followed RESTful conventions properly. He included request validation using Pydantic models, which was a good touch.

ON-SITE FEEDBACK:

System Design (Marcus): LEAN NO HIRE
"His system design was okay but surface-level. When I pushed on scaling questions, he defaulted to 'add more servers' without discussing data partitioning, connection management, or query optimization. He's a good mid-level engineer but I'm not sure he's at the senior level we need. I gave him the same real-time alerting prompt I gave Deepak. He started with a reasonable high-level architecture — message queue, workers, notification service — but when I asked about failure modes, he struggled. What happens when the message queue is full? 'We could increase the queue size.' What happens when a worker crashes mid-processing? 'We restart it.' These aren't wrong answers, but they're not senior answers. A senior engineer would discuss dead letter queues, idempotency, at-least-once delivery semantics, and monitoring. He eventually got to some of these concepts when I prompted him, but the instinct wasn't there yet. I want to be clear: he's not a bad engineer. He's just not where we need him to be for this specific role at this specific time."

Technical Deep-Dive (Amir): NO HIRE
"The pair programming session was rough. He struggled with async Python patterns that are fundamental to our codebase. His PostgreSQL knowledge is functional but not deep — he knows how to write queries but not how to optimize them. I'd worry about him being productive from day one. Specifically, we worked on the same connection pool issue I used with Deepak. James didn't recognize the async context manager pattern and had to look up the asyncpg documentation during the session. I don't penalize people for looking things up — I do it constantly — but the gap between where he is and where our codebase requires him to be is significant. He also wrote a JOIN that produced a Cartesian product without noticing until I pointed out the row count. When I asked about EXPLAIN ANALYZE, he said he'd used it 'a few times' but couldn't interpret the output fluently. Again — functional for a mid-level role, but not the depth we're looking for at senior. I spent the last 15 minutes of the session talking about his learning trajectory, and honestly, he could get there in 12-18 months with the right mentorship. We just can't provide that mentorship right now."

Amir added during the debrief: "I feel bad about the NO HIRE because he's clearly smart and motivated. But I'd be setting him up for failure if I said HIRE. He needs a team with more senior engineers who can mentor him. We need someone who can mentor us."

Product Collaboration (Nadia): HIRE
"Interestingly, his product instincts were the best of the three candidates. He asked great user-focused questions and his approach to technical tradeoffs was pragmatic. If his technical skills were stronger, I'd be enthusiastic. He reframed my product scenario in terms of user outcomes rather than technical deliverables — 'what does the user actually need to accomplish, and what's the simplest thing that would let them do that?' He talked about building MVPs, measuring adoption, and iterating based on data. He mentioned that at Toast, he voluntarily sat with the customer support team for a week to understand common pain points, and then built a small internal tool that reduced ticket volume by 30%. That's the kind of initiative I love to see. His product thinking is genuinely senior-level. If we were hiring a product-focused engineering role, he'd be a strong candidate."

Culture (Priya): LEAN HIRE
"Likeable, genuine, eager to learn. Would be a great team member. But I'm not sure we're the right place for him right now — he needs a role where he can grow into senior, not one that requires senior from day one. He was honest about his growth areas, which I respected. He said he knows his systems design skills need development and he's actively studying — he's been reading 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' and working through distributed systems exercises on the weekends. That kind of self-awareness and initiative is exactly what makes someone eventually great. I told him honestly that our timeline doesn't allow for a ramp-up period and he understood. He asked if we'd consider him for a future role, and I said absolutely."

OVERALL: NO HIRE — 2/4 recommend, consensus is technical depth insufficient for the role
NOTE: Priya suggested we keep his resume for future roles if we open a mid-level backend position. Marcus agreed — if we reach 25+ engineers and can afford dedicated mentorship, James would be an excellent hire.
NOTE 2: Nadia requested that we send James a thoughtful rejection email with specific feedback, since his product instincts were strong and we'd want to maintain the relationship. Marcus agreed to draft it.

CANDIDATE: MIA CHEN (Figma, referral from Jess)
ON-SITE DATE: June 3
INTERVIEWERS: Jess (technical deep-dive), Lina (design collaboration), Nadia (product), Priya (culture)
YEARS OF EXPERIENCE: 6
CURRENT TITLE: Senior Software Engineer, Figma (Canvas Rendering team)

TAKE-HOME REVIEW:
- Score: 9/10
- Reviewers: Jess (primary), Marcus (secondary)
- Beautiful. She built a drag-and-drop layout with smooth animations, proper grid snapping, and three chart types (line, bar, pie) connected to sample data. The code was clean React with custom hooks for the drag-and-drop logic. She used D3 for chart rendering and it was silky smooth. The drag-and-drop used pointer events rather than the HTML5 drag API, which shows she understands the limitations of the native API for complex use cases. Grid snapping included configurable grid size and visual guides during drag operations.
- Code organization was excellent — separate directories for hooks, components, utils, and types. TypeScript types were precise (no `any` types anywhere). She included unit tests for the custom hooks and a few integration tests using Playwright for the drag-and-drop interactions.
- Marcus's secondary review: "I'm a backend engineer reviewing frontend code, so take this with appropriate salt. But even I could follow her code easily. The component hierarchy makes sense, the state management is clean (she used Zustand for global state and local useState for component-level state), and the naming conventions are consistent throughout. The only thing I'd push on is accessibility — the drag-and-drop doesn't have keyboard support. That's a valid omission for a take-home but something to discuss in the on-site."

ON-SITE FEEDBACK:

Technical Deep-Dive (Jess): STRONG HIRE
"She's exactly who we need. Her Figma canvas experience translates directly — she understands rendering performance, event handling, and complex state management. We discussed how to implement real-time data binding for interactive widgets and she immediately proposed a WebSocket-based approach with client-side caching. I've been thinking about this problem for weeks and her solution was better than mine. She drew out the data flow: WebSocket connection managed by a singleton service, incoming messages dispatched to a local store keyed by widget ID, React components subscribe to their specific widget's data slice via a custom hook, and stale data detection using server-sent timestamps. She also discussed reconnection strategies — exponential backoff with jitter, message buffering during disconnection, and state reconciliation on reconnect. When I asked about rendering performance for dashboards with 50+ widgets each receiving real-time updates, she described a virtualization approach: only render widgets in the viewport, use requestAnimationFrame for batch updates, and debounce re-renders at the store level. She's clearly thought deeply about these problems at Figma. We also discussed testing strategies for WebSocket-based features — she uses mock WebSocket servers in tests and has built test utilities for simulating network failures. Her code review style is thorough but kind — she showed me examples of PR reviews she'd done at Figma (with permission), and they were constructive and specific."

Jess added: "I want to flag something important. Mia mentioned she has experience with WebGL rendering from her work on the Figma canvas. Our current dashboard is pure DOM/SVG, but if we ever want to do high-performance data visualization with large datasets, WebGL rendering would be a game-changer. She's not just solving today's problems — she opens up possibilities for tomorrow's product."

Design Collaboration (Lina): STRONG HIRE
"I've never had such a productive collaboration conversation in an interview. She looked at my designs and gave specific, actionable feedback — 'this drag handle should be larger for touch targets,' 'the empty state should suggest common layouts.' She thinks like a designer AND an engineer. She's going to make our product significantly better. I showed her three mock-ups for the dashboard builder: a blank canvas approach, a template gallery approach, and a guided wizard approach. She evaluated each from both a technical feasibility and UX perspective. For the blank canvas: 'technically simplest, but highest user friction — you're asking users to start from zero.' For the template gallery: 'good starting point, but the templates need to be genuinely useful, not just demo content — otherwise users click a template, see it doesn't match their data, and leave.' For the guided wizard: 'best for onboarding new users, but power users will find it frustrating after the first time — you'd need a way to skip it.' She then proposed a hybrid: template gallery as the default entry point, with a 'start from scratch' option for power users, and contextual guidance that appears only when the user seems stuck (e.g., empty dashboard after 30 seconds). That's a better solution than any of us had come up with individually. She also noticed inconsistencies in my spacing system — I was using 4px in some places and 5px in others — and suggested standardizing on an 8px grid. Small thing, but it shows attention to detail."

Lina also mentioned in the debrief: "I've been the only design-minded person on the team for a year. Having an engineer who genuinely cares about design quality and can provide informed feedback would change my day-to-day experience dramatically. I'm being selfish here, but I really want her on the team."

Product (Nadia): STRONG HIRE
"She understood the problem from a product perspective — not just 'build a widget' but 'help non-technical users get answers from data.' She talked about progressive disclosure, guided setup flows, and template-based starting points. This is someone who cares about the user, not just the code. I walked her through our current user journey for creating a dashboard, and she identified three friction points I'd already documented in our product backlog — without being told about them. The biggest one: our current flow requires users to write SQL to connect data, which immediately excludes 80% of our target users. She proposed a visual query builder with a natural language fallback — 'show me revenue by region for the last 6 months' — which is exactly the feature we've been discussing for Q3. She also raised an important point about data freshness expectations: 'if the dashboard says revenue is $1.2M, users need to know whether that's as of right now, as of this morning, or as of last week. Stale data presented as current data is worse than no data.' She suggested a subtle timestamp and freshness indicator on each widget. These aren't revolutionary ideas individually, but the fact that she raised them unprompted in an interview setting tells me she'll raise similar concerns in everyday product discussions."

Culture (Priya): STRONG HIRE
"She's relocating from SF to be closer to family in Boston. This isn't a flight-of-fancy move — she's committed. She asked about our values, our decision-making process, and how we handle disagreements. She told me about a time she pushed back on a Figma product decision and was ultimately proven right. She's not afraid to voice an opinion. The specific story: the Figma team wanted to ship a canvas feature with a known performance regression on large files, arguing it affected less than 5% of users. Mia argued that the affected 5% were their most engaged power users and that shipping a regression would damage trust disproportionately. She built a prototype that solved the performance issue using a different rendering approach, presented it in a team meeting, and the team adopted her solution. The feature shipped without the regression. What I liked about how she told this story: she gave credit to her teammates for being open to her alternative approach, rather than framing it as 'I was right and they were wrong.' She also asked thoughtful questions about our diversity and inclusion efforts. I was honest that we're a small team and haven't formalized D&I programs, but that it's important to us as we grow. She offered to help — she was on the D&I committee at Figma and helped design their engineering interview rubric to reduce bias. That's the kind of initiative we need."

Priya added: "Her references were outstanding. Her skip-level manager at Figma called her 'one of the top five engineers I've managed in my career' and said the team was genuinely sad to see her go. Her peer reference described her as 'the person everyone wants on their project because she makes everything better.' Both references specifically mentioned her communication skills and her ability to bridge engineering and design."

OVERALL: STRONG HIRE — 4/4 strong hires (unanimous)

SECTION 1: INFRASTRUCTURE OVERVIEW

This document provides a comprehensive review of Verdana Analytics's production infrastructure as of May 13, 2025 — two days before our production deployment. The purpose is to document the current architecture, identify known risks, and establish operational baselines. This document supersedes the draft infrastructure plan from March 2025 (ADR-2025-009) and reflects all changes made during the beta period.

For context, Verdana Analytics is a data integration platform that ingests event streams from customer data sources, processes them through a real-time pipeline, and exposes analytics via REST API and WebSocket subscriptions. The architecture prioritizes low-latency event processing and horizontal scalability.

ARCHITECTURE DIAGRAM (text representation):

```
Users → CloudFront CDN → ALB (Application Load Balancer)
                              ↓
                    ECS Fargate (API containers, 4 tasks)
                              ↓
                    ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                    ↓                   ↓
            RDS PostgreSQL 15     Redis Cluster
            (db.r6g.xlarge)       (cache.r6g.large)
            Primary + 1 Read      3 nodes
            Replica
                    ↓
            Kafka (MSK)
            3 brokers
            t3.large
```

REGION: us-east-1 (primary), eu-west-1 (CDN + future expansion)

Infrastructure-as-code: All resources are managed via Terraform (v1.7.2) in the `infra/terraform/` repo. State is stored in S3 with DynamoDB locking. Jess ran `terraform plan` on May 12 and confirmed zero drift between the state file and actual AWS resources.

Kevin: "I want to call out that every resource shown in the diagram above is tagged with `Environment=production`, `Team=platform`, and `CostCenter=eng-infra`. We set up AWS Cost Explorer views by tag, so we can track actual vs. projected spend starting day one."

Jess: "Also worth noting — the Terraform modules are pinned to specific versions. No floating refs. I updated the lockfile on May 10 and verified checksums."

VPC layout:
- VPC CIDR: 10.0.0.0/16
- Public subnets: 10.0.1.0/24 (us-east-1a), 10.0.2.0/24 (us-east-1b)
- Private subnets: 10.0.10.0/24 (us-east-1a), 10.0.11.0/24 (us-east-1b)
- NAT Gateways: 2 (one per AZ, for private subnet egress)
- Flow logs: enabled, exported to CloudWatch Logs group /vpc/verdana-production

DNS: verdana-analytics.io hosted on Route 53. API endpoint: api.verdana-analytics.io. Status page: status.verdana-analytics.io (hosted on Statuspage.io, separate from our infra).

***

SECTION 2: COMPUTE — ECS FARGATE

Configuration:
- Service: verdana-api
- Task count: 4 (desired), auto-scaling 2-16
- CPU per task: 1 vCPU
- Memory per task: 2 GB
- Container image: verdana-api:v1.0.0-rc3
- Health check: GET /health, 30s interval, 3 unhealthy threshold
- Deployment strategy: rolling (minimum healthy 50%)
- Task execution role: verdana-ecs-task-execution (allows ECR pull, CloudWatch Logs write)
- Task role: verdana-api-task-role (allows RDS access, Redis access, MSK access, S3 read/write to verdana-assets bucket)

Auto-scaling rules:
- Scale up: CPU utilization > 60% for 3 minutes → add 2 tasks
- Scale down: CPU utilization < 20% for 10 minutes → remove 1 task
- Maximum: 16 tasks
- Minimum: 2 tasks (cost savings during off-hours)
- Cooldown period: 300 seconds between scaling actions

Kevin walked through the scaling configuration in detail during the review:

Kevin: "The 60% threshold for scale-up might seem aggressive, but we found during testing that our API containers exhibit a steep latency curve once CPU hits 70%. The 3-minute evaluation window filters out transient spikes — like when a customer does a bulk import and CPU jumps to 75% for 30 seconds. We don't want to scale for those."

Amir: "What about memory-based scaling? Some of our aggregation queries hold intermediate results in memory."

Kevin: "Good question. I looked at this. During the load test on May 11, peak memory usage per task was 1.3 GB out of 2 GB. That's 65%, and it only lasted about 90 seconds during the heaviest aggregation workload. CPU consistently correlated better with actual load. If we see memory-driven bottlenecks in production, we can add a memory-based scaling policy — it's a one-line Terraform change."

Amir: "Makes sense. Let's revisit after two weeks of production data."

Cost estimate: $0.04048/vCPU/hour × 4 tasks × 24h × 30d = $116.58/month baseline
With auto-scaling headroom (est. avg 6 tasks): ~$175/month

Container build pipeline:
- Source: GitHub (verdana-analytics/api repo, main branch)
- CI: GitHub Actions (.github/workflows/build.yml)
- Build: Docker multi-stage build (builder + runtime)
- Registry: AWS ECR (verdana-api repository, lifecycle policy retains last 30 images)
- Scan: ECR image scanning enabled (no critical or high vulnerabilities in v1.0.0-rc3)
- Deploy: `aws ecs update-service` triggered by CI on tag push (tags matching v*.*.*)

David: "I ran Trivy against the rc3 image yesterday. Zero critical, zero high, two medium (both in transitive dependencies of the Node.js PDF renderer — not reachable from our code path). I'm comfortable shipping with these."

Notes:
- Kevin tested the auto-scaling by running a load test at 3AM on May 11. Tasks scaled from 2 to 8 within 4 minutes. Scale-down took 12 minutes (the 10-minute cool-down is intentional to avoid thrashing).

Kevin: "The load test used Apache Bench with 500 concurrent connections hitting the /api/v1/events endpoint for 15 minutes. Peak request rate was 2,400 req/s across the 8 tasks. No 5xx errors. P99 latency peaked at 280ms during the scale-up window, then settled to 45ms once all 8 tasks were healthy."

Jess: "I was monitoring the ALB target group during that test. The deregistration delay is set to 30 seconds, which means during scale-down, in-flight requests have 30 seconds to complete before the task is killed. We saw zero dropped connections during scale-down."

- Container startup time is 14 seconds (measured from task launch to first health check pass). This is acceptable for scaling events but too slow for cold start scenarios. We should investigate prewarming in Q3.

Priya: "14 seconds seems long. Is that the JVM startup or something?"

Kevin: "No, we're Python — it's the combination of pulling the container layer cache (about 4 seconds), starting the uvicorn workers (about 3 seconds), and waiting for the first health check interval (the first check fires at about 7 seconds after task launch, but you need 1 successful check). I've opened a ticket to look at reducing the health check initial delay. But honestly, for scaling events — where we're adding capacity to an already-running service — 14 seconds is fine."

- Logging: all container logs go to CloudWatch Logs group /ecs/verdana-api. Retention: 30 days. Costs: ~$8/month at current log volume.
- Log format: structured JSON (timestamp, level, request_id, account_id, message, duration_ms)
- Correlation: every inbound request gets a UUID request_id propagated through all downstream calls (Redis, Postgres, Kafka)

***

SECTION 3: DATABASE — RDS POSTGRESQL 15

Configuration:
- Instance class: db.r6g.xlarge (4 vCPU, 32 GB RAM)
- Storage: 200 GB gp3 (3,000 IOPS, 125 MB/s throughput)
- Multi-AZ: Yes (standby in us-east-1b)
- Read replica: 1 (db.r6g.large, 2 vCPU, 16 GB RAM)
- Backup: Daily automated, 7-day retention
- Backup window: 03:00-04:00 UTC (off-peak for US customers)
- Maintenance window: Sun 06:00-07:00 UTC
- Encryption: AES-256 (AWS KMS, key alias: alias/verdana-rds)
- Parameter group: Custom (shared_buffers=8GB, work_mem=256MB, max_connections=200)
- PostgreSQL extensions enabled: pg_stat_statements (query performance), pgcrypto (field-level encryption), uuid-ossp

Priya: "I want to flag that we're using the custom parameter group with shared_buffers at 8 GB. That's 25% of the 32 GB instance memory, which is the PostgreSQL recommendation. But if we ever downsize the instance, we need to adjust this proportionally or we'll get OOM kills."

Marcus: "Good callout. Let's add that to the runbook as a caveat for any instance resize."

Kevin: "Already noted in the Terraform variable description, but I'll add it to the runbook too."

Connection management:
- pgBouncer deployed as a sidecar container in each ECS task
- Pool mode: transaction (connections returned to pool after each transaction)
- Pool size per sidecar: 25 connections
- Total pool capacity: 25 × 4 tasks = 100 connections (at baseline task count)
- Reserve connections for admin: 5 (separate from pool, for migrations and manual queries)
- Connection string uses IAM authentication (no stored passwords)

Performance baseline (from load testing, May 11):
- Query latency (simple SELECT by primary key): 2-5ms
- Query latency (aggregation with GROUP BY on indexed columns): 15-40ms
- Query latency (complex JOIN + aggregation across 2-3 tables): 50-120ms
- Query latency (full-text search on payload_jsonb): 80-200ms depending on document size
- Connection pool: 100 connections (via pgBouncer sidecar)
- Maximum observed concurrent queries: 45
- Peak QPS during load test: 1,200 queries/second
- Replication lag to read replica: <50ms under load (measured via pg_stat_replication)

Amir: "The 50-120ms range for complex JOINs is acceptable for our analytics endpoints. Those are the dashboard queries that populate charts — users expect sub-second, and with the Redis cache in front, most of those will be served from cache anyway."

Cost estimate:
- Primary: $0.464/hour × 730h = $338.72/month
- Read replica: $0.232/hour × 730h = $169.36/month
- Storage: $0.08/GB × 200GB = $16/month
- IOPS: included in gp3 base (3,000 free)
- Backup: ~$5/month
- Data transfer (cross-AZ replication): ~$3/month estimated
- Total database: ~$529/month

Schema overview:
- accounts (id, name, created_at, plan, billing_status) — 200 rows expected year 1
- users (id, account_id, email, role, created_at) — ~2,400 rows expected (12 users/account avg)
- connectors (id, account_id, source_type, config_encrypted, status, last_sync) — ~600 rows (3 connectors/account avg)
- events (id, account_id, connector_id, event_type, payload_jsonb, created_at) — PARTITIONED by month, expected 50M rows/month at scale
- queries (id, account_id, query_json, created_at, execution_time_ms) — audit trail, ~10K rows/month initially
- webhooks (id, account_id, url, filter_json, status) — ~500 rows expected

Priya provided additional context on the events table design:

Priya: "The events table is by far our largest table. The payload_jsonb column stores the raw event data from customer connectors — it can be anywhere from 200 bytes to 15 KB per event depending on the source type. Salesforce events tend to be large (5-15 KB), while Stripe webhook events are typically under 1 KB. We benchmarked both extremes during load testing."

Partitioning strategy:
- events table is partitioned by month using PostgreSQL declarative partitioning
- Each partition is a separate table (events_2025_05, events_2025_06, etc.)
- Partition pruning ensures queries only scan relevant months
- Old partitions can be archived to S3 via pg_dump after 6 months
- Partition creation is automated via a cron job (runs on the 25th of each month to create the next month's partition)
- Amir wrote a migration script (db/migrations/003_create_partition.sql) that handles partition creation idempotently

Kevin: "I tested the partition archival process last week. Dumping a partition with 50M rows to S3 takes about 12 minutes using pg_dump with compression. Restoring from S3 takes about 25 minutes. Both operations can run without locking the active partitions."

Indexing:
- events: B-tree on (account_id, created_at), GIN on payload_jsonb
- connectors: B-tree on (account_id, source_type)
- queries: B-tree on (account_id, created_at)
- users: B-tree on (account_id), unique on (email)
- webhooks: B-tree on (account_id, status)

Known concern: The GIN index on payload_jsonb will grow large as event volume increases. At 50M rows/month, the index could reach 10-15 GB per partition. We should monitor index size and consider partial indexes on frequently queried JSON paths.

Amir: "I've already identified the three most common JSON path queries from our beta users: `$.event_type`, `$.source.id`, and `$.metadata.tags`. If the GIN index becomes a problem, we can create partial B-tree indexes on these paths. I'll file a ticket to revisit this at the 30-day mark."

Migration management:
- Tool: Alembic (Python)
- All migrations in db/migrations/ directory
- Pre-launch migrations applied as of May 12: 14 total (001 through 014)
- Migration 014 added the per-account connection limit check (implemented May 13 — see Section 8, Risk #2)
- Rollback tested for all migrations through 010. Migrations 011-014 have forward-only changes (adding columns, creating partitions) that are safe without rollback.

***

SECTION 4: CACHING — REDIS CLUSTER

Configuration:
- Cluster mode: enabled (3 shards, 1 replica per shard)
- Node type: cache.r6g.large (2 vCPU, 13 GB RAM)
- Total memory: 39 GB across cluster (6 nodes × 6.5 GB usable per node)
- Encryption in transit: TLS enabled
- Encryption at rest: enabled (AWS KMS)
- Eviction policy: allkeys-lru
- Snapshot: daily, 1-day retention
- Maintenance window: Mon 05:00-06:00 UTC
- Parameter group: maxmemory-policy=allkeys-lru, timeout=300, tcp-keepalive=60

Usage patterns:
1. API response caching — TTL 60s for frequently accessed queries (key format: `cache:v1:{account_id}:{query_hash}`)
2. Rate limiting — sliding window counters per API key (key format: `ratelimit:{api_key}:{window}`)
3. Real-time streaming state — WebSocket subscription metadata (key format: `ws:sub:{subscription_id}`)
4. Session storage — user authentication tokens (key format: `session:{session_id}`, TTL 24h)

Amir: "I want to walk through the cache invalidation strategy. When an event is ingested for account X, we invalidate all cache keys matching `cache:v1:{X}:*`. This is a coarse invalidation — it clears all cached queries for that account. It's simple and correct, but it means high-write accounts will see lower cache hit rates. For our beta users, the average hit rate was 72%. I expect it'll be in the 60-75% range at launch."

Jess: "Are we using Redis Cluster's hash tags for the invalidation scan?"

Amir: "Yes, the account_id is wrapped in curly braces in the key — `cache:v1:{account_id}:{hash}` — so all keys for an account land on the same shard. The SCAN + DEL for invalidation only hits one shard."

Current utilization: ~2% (pre-launch, minimal data)
Expected utilization at 200 accounts: 15-25%
Memory per account (estimated): 50 MB average (mostly cached query results)

Cost estimate: $0.252/hour × 6 nodes × 730h = $1,103.76/month

Kevin's note: Redis Cluster is overkill for our current scale. A single cache.r6g.large node ($184/month) would suffice for the first 6 months. But the cluster setup avoids a migration later, and the incremental cost ($920/month) is worth avoiding downtime from a mid-growth migration. I recommend keeping the cluster.

Marcus's response: Agree. Keep the cluster. The cost is acceptable relative to the operational risk of migrating under load.

Jess: "For what it's worth, I tested the failover behavior last week. When I killed the primary node on shard 1, the replica promoted in 8 seconds. The API saw about 200ms of elevated latency during the failover but zero errors — the Redis client library (redis-py with retry on timeout) handled it cleanly."

Kevin: "Good to know. I'll document that in the runbook."

***

SECTION 5: STREAMING — AMAZON MSK (MANAGED KAFKA)

Configuration:
- Cluster name: verdana-streaming
- Kafka version: 3.5.1
- Brokers: 3 (t3.large instances, 2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM each)
- Storage: 100 GB per broker (gp3)
- Replication factor: 3
- Min in-sync replicas: 2
- Log retention: 7 days (168 hours)
- Compression: lz4 (producer-side)
- Authentication: IAM (no SASL/SCRAM)

Topics:
- verdana.events.ingest — raw events from connectors (partitioned by account_id, 12 partitions)
- verdana.events.processed — transformed events ready for storage (12 partitions)
- verdana.streaming.subscriptions — WebSocket subscription management (3 partitions)
- verdana.webhooks.delivery — outbound webhook events (6 partitions)

Priya: "The partition counts were chosen based on our expected consumer parallelism. For the events topics, 12 partitions lets us run up to 12 consumer instances. At our launch scale we'll have 4 consumers, so there's room to scale 3x without repartitioning."

Amir: "Why 6 partitions for webhooks?"

Priya: "Webhook delivery is I/O-bound — each delivery involves an outbound HTTP call that can take 1-5 seconds. More partitions let us parallelize the HTTP calls across more consumer threads. Six felt right for launch. If webhook volume grows, we can bump to 12 without impacting the other topics."

Consumer group configuration:
- verdana-event-processor (consumes from verdana.events.ingest, produces to verdana.events.processed)
  - Instance count: 4 (one per ECS task)
  - Auto-commit: disabled (manual offset commit after successful processing)
  - Max poll records: 500
  - Session timeout: 30s
  - Heartbeat interval: 10s

- verdana-storage-writer (consumes from verdana.events.processed, writes to PostgreSQL)
  - Instance count: 2
  - Batch size: 100 events per INSERT (using PostgreSQL COPY for bulk inserts)
  - Flush interval: 5 seconds (whichever comes first — batch size or interval)

- verdana-webhook-dispatcher (consumes from verdana.webhooks.delivery, makes outbound HTTP calls)
  - Instance count: 2
  - Retry policy: 3 attempts with exponential backoff (1s, 5s, 25s)
  - Dead letter topic: verdana.webhooks.dlq (failed after 3 retries)

Throughput baseline:
- Producer throughput: 10,000 events/second sustained (tested over 30-minute window)
- Consumer throughput: 15,000 events/second sustained
- End-to-end latency (produce → consume → store): 45ms p95
- Consumer lag: <100 messages under normal load
- Peak throughput tested: 25,000 events/second (consumers kept up, no lag accumulation)

Priya: "The 10K sustained number is our target for year-1 peak. The 25K number was a burst test. At 25K/s, we started seeing some GC pauses on the t3.large brokers — nothing critical, but if we need sustained throughput above 15K/s, we should upgrade the brokers to m5.xlarge."

Kevin: "Noted. I'll add a scaling trigger — if we sustain >12K/s for a week, we upgrade brokers."

Cost estimate:
- Broker instances: $0.0456/hour × 3 × 730h = $99.86/month
- Storage: $0.10/GB × 300GB total = $30/month
- Data transfer: ~$20/month estimated
- Total Kafka: ~$150/month

Monitoring:
- Consumer lag alerts: >1,000 messages for 5 minutes → PagerDuty
- Broker disk usage: >80% → PagerDuty
- Under-replicated partitions: >0 for 5 minutes → PagerDuty
- Producer error rate: >1% for 3 minutes → Slack #ops-alerts
- Consumer group rebalance events → Slack #ops-alerts (informational)

Priya: "One more thing on Kafka — I set up a compacted topic `verdana.connectors.state` that stores the last-known state of each connector. This is used by the API to show connector status without hitting the database. It's a small optimization but it eliminates a common query pattern."

SECTION 6: NETWORKING AND SECURITY

David presented this section during the review.

David: "I want to cover the security posture of the production environment. This is a summary — the full security review is in a separate document (SEC-2025-003) that I'll share with the team after launch."

TLS/SSL:

WAF rules (AWS WAF on ALB):

- XSS rule set: AWS Managed Rules — XSS
- Geographic blocking: none (we serve globally)
- Bot control: not enabled (costs $10/month, revisit after launch based on bot traffic patterns)

David: "The WAF rules are intentionally minimal. We're relying more on application-layer security — input validation, parameterized queries, RBAC — than on WAF. The WAF is a safety net, not the primary defense."

Secrets management:
- All secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager
- Secrets are injected into ECS tasks via task definition environment variables (referencing Secrets Manager ARNs)
- Secrets list: database connection string, Redis auth token, Datadog API key, Stripe API key, SendGrid API key
- Rotation: database credentials rotate every 90 days (automated via Lambda function)
- No secrets in environment variables, no secrets in code, no secrets in Terraform state (state is encrypted but still)

IAM:
- Principle of least privilege applied to all roles
- ECS task role can only access: specific RDS instance, specific ElastiCache cluster, specific MSK cluster, specific S3 bucket
- No wildcard permissions
- IAM Access Analyzer enabled — last finding was March 2 (resolved)

David: "I ran an IAM Access Analyzer report this morning. Zero external access findings. The only cross-account access is the Datadog integration role, which is scoped to CloudWatch read-only."

Key dashboards:

3. "Streaming Pipeline" — Kafka throughput (produce/consume), consumer lag by topic and consumer group, event processing rate, end-to-end latency
4. "Business Metrics" — signups, API calls per account, active accounts, connector sync status (updated hourly via a scheduled query)
5. "Infrastructure Overview" — CPU/memory/network for all services, disk usage, container count, scaling events

| Database connections | >80% pool for 3 min | P2 | PagerDuty (SMS) |

Runbook: Notion page "Verdana On-Call Runbook" — includes diagnostic steps for each alert type, escalation procedures, and rollback instructions.

Runbook sections:
1. API error rate spike — check Datadog APM for error traces, check recent deployments, check database connectivity
2. Database connection exhaustion — check pgBouncer stats, identify long-running queries via pg_stat_activity, kill if necessary
3. Kafka consumer lag — check consumer health, restart consumer group if needed, check for poison messages
4. Rollback procedure — revert ECS service to previous task definition revision (one CLI command)
5. Database failover — automatic via RDS Multi-AZ, manual promotion of read replica if needed
6. Full outage — incident response template, communication plan, status page update procedure

SECTION 9: KNOWN RISKS AND MITIGATIONS

Marcus led the risk discussion. Each risk was discussed and assigned an owner.

1. Single-region deployment — if us-east-1 has an outage, we're down
   Mitigation: eu-west-1 is prepared for failover but not actively serving traffic. Manual failover procedure exists (documented in runbook section 6). Target for automated multi-region: Q4.
   Owner: Kevin
   Probability: Low (AWS SLA 99.99%)
   Impact: High (full outage)

   Kevin: "The eu-west-1 setup includes a VPC, subnets, and an ALB — but no running resources. If us-east-1 goes down, we'd need to spin up ECS tasks, promote a cross-region read replica (which we don't have yet), and update DNS. Realistically, that's a 30-60 minute manual process. Automated multi-region requires cross-region replication for both RDS and Redis, which is a Q4 project."

   Jess: "Should we at least set up the cross-region RDS replica now? It's about $170/month for a db.r6g.large in eu-west-1."

   Marcus: "Not for launch. Let's revisit after we have paying customers and revenue to justify the cost. Add it to the Q3 planning doc."

2. Connection pool exhaustion under concurrent heavy queries
   Mitigation: per-account connection limits (implemented May 13), query timeout of 60s, read replica for batch operations.
   Owner: Amir
   Probability: Medium
   Impact: Medium (degraded service for one account, not all)

   Amir: "The per-account limit is 10 connections. If one account runs 10 concurrent heavy queries, they'll hit the limit and get a 429 response. Other accounts are unaffected because the connections are isolated via pgBouncer pool partitioning. I tested this by running 15 concurrent queries for one account — queries 11-15 got 429s, other accounts continued normally."

3. Kafka consumer restart cold-start
   Mitigation: consumers checkpoint offsets every 5 seconds. Restart from last checkpoint. Maximum data loss: 5 seconds of events.
   Owner: Priya
   Probability: Medium (container restarts happen)
   Impact: Low (events are replayed from Kafka, at-least-once delivery)

   Priya: "To be precise — this isn't data loss, it's delayed processing. When a consumer restarts, it resumes from the last committed offset. Any events produced in the last 5 seconds before the crash will be re-consumed. Our event processing is idempotent (we use the event UUID as the database primary key), so duplicate processing just results in a no-op INSERT ON CONFLICT."

4. No disaster recovery testing
   Mitigation: Scheduled DR test for June 15. Will simulate primary DB failure and test failover to read replica.
   Owner: Kevin
   Probability: N/A (this is a process gap, not a failure mode)
   Impact: Unknown until tested

   Marcus: "This is the one that keeps me up at night. We've never tested a full failover. Kevin, make sure the June 15 test includes: RDS failover, Redis failover, Kafka broker replacement, and ECS task failure recovery. I want the full matrix."

   Kevin: "Will do. I'll write up a test plan by May 30 and share it for review."

DECISION 1: PRICING AT $29/MO (May 1)

Original rationale: Undercut Rivalytics at $49 by 41%. Capture mid-market with lower SMB churn. Tomoko's model showed 71% margins, 4-month CAC payback. The $29 price point was selected after Tomoko modeled three tiers ($19, $29, $39) and Derek ran informal pricing sensitivity with 6 prospects. 4 of 6 said $29 was "very reasonable" for the feature set described. $19 was perceived as "suspiciously cheap" by 2 respondents. $39 had pushback from 3 respondents who said it was too close to Rivalytics for a new entrant.

What actually happened: Rivalytics dropped to $19 after 16 days. Our price advantage evaporated. We're now the premium option. The $29 price point that was designed to be an aggressive undercut is now a 53% premium over the incumbent.

Assessment: The pricing RATIONALE was sound — undercutting the incumbent is a valid entry strategy. The failure was in our risk analysis. We rated a Rivalytics price drop below $29 as "low probability, less than 5% in 12 months." We based this on their cost structure, which was correct — their pre-Series D cost structure couldn't sustain $19. But we didn't account for the possibility of new funding specifically to enable aggressive pricing.

[00:01:42] MARCUS OKAFOR: To be fair, we couldn't have known about the Series D. That deal was announced simultaneously with the price drop. There were no signals. I actually looked back through their blog, their LinkedIn, their job postings — nothing suggested a major funding round was in progress. They even had a blog post in April about "sustainable growth" which, in hindsight, was probably cover for the fundraise.

[00:01:50] NADIA REIS: True. But the lesson is: when your strategy depends on a competitor NOT doing something, you need a plan for when they do it anyway. We had the sensitivity analysis but we treated the worst case as too unlikely to plan for concretely. In the May 1 pricing meeting, I actually asked "what if they match us at $29?" and we discussed that for about ten minutes. But nobody asked "what if they go BELOW us?" — and that's the scenario that materialized.

[00:02:05] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: What was the actual financial impact on us?

[00:02:08] NADIA REIS: Derek's latest pipeline update: of the 8 active prospects as of May 30, 4 have gone cold since the Rivalytics announcement. One explicitly said they're going with Rivalytics. The other 3 are "evaluating." Of our 16 paid accounts, we've had 2 inquiries about price matching — neither has churned yet, but they're asking. The pricing adjustment to $24 monthly and $19 annual seems to have stabilized the existing base. No churn in the 3 days since the adjustment.

[00:02:35] TOMOKO ISHIKAWA: Can I add some numbers to that? I ran the updated model yesterday. At the original $29 price with the pre-Rivalytics pipeline, we were projecting $14,200 MRR by end of June — that's based on 16 existing accounts plus a 60% close rate on the 8 prospects. With the new pricing and the pipeline contraction, the updated projection is $9,400 MRR by end of June. That's a $4,800 monthly delta, which compounds over time. The 4-month CAC payback is now looking more like 6.5 months at $24, or 8 months at $19 annual if customers choose that tier.

[00:03:02] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: That's a meaningful hit but not existential. We have runway through February on the current burn rate. Tomoko, does the revised pricing change the runway calculation?

At $19, Derek's own research showed that 2 of 6 prospects thought the product seemed "suspiciously cheap." We're an analytics platform handling business-critical data. There's a floor below which price signals low quality. I think $24 is close to that floor for our target market.

DECISION 2: CUTTING THE DASHBOARD FROM LAUNCH (May 4)

Original rationale: Ship faster with API-only. Beachhead customers are technical buyers who want API access. Dashboard is an expansion play, not an acquisition play. Deferred to Q2, targeting July 15. The decision was made in the May 4 roadmap prioritization meeting. Lina presented three dashboard scope options: (A) full-featured dashboard with drag-and-drop builder, collaborative editing, and 12 widget types — estimated 6 weeks; (B) standard dashboard with fixed layouts, 6 widget types, basic data binding — estimated 3 weeks; (C) minimal dashboard with grid layout, line charts, and number widgets only — estimated 2 weeks. The team chose to cut all three options and ship API-only.

What actually happened: The dashboard became the #1 user request from day one. 62 dashboard requests in 2 weeks. Three trial accounts explicitly said they won't convert without it. Two paid accounts have expansion blocked by the dashboard gap. Rivalytics just shipped an enhanced dashboard builder. Our July 15 target has been accelerated to June 18 but even that feels late.

And we did — from day one. I have the Intercom logs — the first dashboard request came in 4 hours after launch, and we've been getting about 4-5 per day ever since.

[00:04:32] NADIA REIS: You were right, Lina. The question is whether the tradeoff was still correct at the time.

[00:04:38] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: I think it was. If we had waited 4-6 weeks for the dashboard, we would have launched on June 15 instead of May 15. By then, Rivalytics would already be at $19 with enhanced dashboards. We'd be launching a $29 product with dashboards into a market where the incumbent is $19 with dashboards. At least by launching early, we got 16 paid accounts and proved the product has value. Those 16 accounts are real revenue and real validation. We wouldn't have that if we'd waited.

[00:05:00] LINA CHOI: I agree the timing was right. But what if we had shipped a MINIMAL dashboard? Not the full vision — just basic charts on a grid. [00:07:12] PRIYA CHANDRASEKARAN: That's a good insight, Marcus. And honestly, I agreed with you at the time. The lesson for all of us is: when user research says "5 of 8 prospects want X," and our designer is raising a flag, we should listen harder. Even if the general principle says "cut scope."

Assessment: The decision to prioritize speed was directionally correct. The failure was in scoping — we should have included a minimal dashboard (basic charts on a grid, no query builder, static data binding) as a 2-week launch addition rather than cutting the entire feature. When user research clearly signals demand (5 of 8 prospects), the right response is to find the minimum viable scope, not to defer entirely. DECISION 3: COMPETITIVE POSITIONING (May 7)

Original rationale: Three-pillar positioning — (1) real-time streaming vs batch ETL, (2) price advantage ($29 vs $49), (3) API-first vs dashboard-first. Derek built a competitive battlecard around these three pillars. The battlecard was used in 11 prospect conversations between May 7 and May 31. Sales messaging was scripted around the three-pillar framework, with pillar 1 (real-time) as the technical differentiator, pillar 2 (price) as the business case, and pillar 3 (API-first) as the developer appeal.

What actually happened: Pillar 2 (price) has collapsed — they're cheaper now. Pillar 3 (API-first vs dashboard-first) has weakened — they have enhanced dashboards and we don't have any. The "API-first" positioning now reads as "no dashboard" to non-technical buyers. Pillar 1 (real-time) is still valid but now needs to carry the entire argument by itself.

[00:09:30] MARCUS OKAFOR: Pillar 1 is genuinely strong though. Their "Rivalytics Live" at 15-minute freshness is not competitive with our sub-200ms streaming. For any real-time use case, they're not even in the conversation. And their architecture can't get there without a ground-up rebuild — which is why they just posted 4 streaming infrastructure jobs. I've looked at the job descriptions — they're hiring for Kafka, Flink, and real-time pipeline engineers. That's a 12-18 month buildout at minimum. They can't fake sub-second freshness with polling and caching.

[00:09:52] JESS THORNTON: I can confirm that from the technical side. Their API documentation still shows batch endpoints with polling intervals. Even their "Live" tier is just a shorter polling interval — it's not true streaming. They don't have WebSocket support, they don't have event-driven updates, they don't have incremental computation. Their data pipeline is fundamentally batch-oriented. You can put lipstick on a batch pipeline, but it's still a batch pipeline.

[00:10:10] NADIA REIS: I hear you both, and I agree the technical advantage is real. But here's the honest truth from the user interviews: only 3 of our 16 paid accounts specifically cited real-time as the reason they chose us. That's 19%. The other 81% came for the price, the API quality, or both. If we lose the price argument, we need those users to discover the real-time advantage organically, which means we need use cases and documentation that SHOW why 15 minutes isn't good enough.

[00:10:35] DEREK VASQUEZ: Can I jump in here? I've been on the calls with these customers. The issue isn't that real-time doesn't matter to them — it's that they don't know it matters yet. They haven't hit the moment yet where they need sub-second data and can't get it. Once they're in production and their ops team is relying on the data for real-time decisions, the 15-minute gap becomes a problem. We just haven't had customers long enough for that to happen organically.

[00:10:58] NADIA REIS: That's a great point, Derek. Which is exactly why we need the case studies. We need to find the customers who ARE using real-time data and tell their story.

Assessment: The three-pillar positioning was sound but over-indexed on price as a differentiator. Future positioning should be anchored on defensible technical advantages, not on pricing that can be matched or undercut. The real-time advantage is architecturally defensible (12-18 month rebuild for Rivalytics), while price and feature parity are not.

DECISION 4: LAUNCH DATE OF MAY 15 (May 10)

Original rationale: Ship on the earliest feasible date to establish market presence. We had originally targeted June 1, but the team felt confident the API was production-ready by May 12. Priya pushed to accelerate to May 15 to capture the spring buying cycle. The 3-week pull-in required cutting the dashboard (as discussed) and shipping with limited documentation (API reference only, no tutorials or guides).

What actually happened: The May 15 launch gave us a 16-day head start on the Rivalytics price drop. In those 16 days, we acquired 16 paid accounts and 31 trial accounts. If we had stuck to the June 1 date, we would have launched into the teeth of the Rivalytics response with zero existing customer base. The early launch also gave us real market feedback that informed our Saturday crisis response — we knew our customers, we knew their use cases, and we could make pricing decisions based on real data instead of projections.

Assessment: Launching on May 15 instead of June 1 was the strongest decision of the period. It created market presence, generated real revenue and data, and gave us 16 days of head start before the competitive landscape shifted. The tradeoff was launching without a dashboard and with limited documentation, both of which created customer friction — but the alternative (launching later into a worse competitive environment with zero customer base) was clearly worse.

We assumed Rivalytics would behave predictably based on their existing cost structure. We didn't model scenario trees where they get new resources. Third, speed over scope. We consistently chose to go faster by cutting scope, which was mostly right — but we cut too aggressively in some areas.

A sensitivity analysis that shows "5% chance of competitor price drop" should make us prepare for that 5% scenario, not dismiss it.

| Decision | Made On | Original Score | Hindsight Score | Key Learning |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Price at $29 | May 1 | 8/10 | 5/10 | Strategy based on competitor NOT acting is fragile |
| Cut dashboard | May 4 | 7/10 | 4/10 | Scope to minimum viable, not zero |
| Comp positioning | May 7 | 8/10 | 6/10 | Anchor on defensible tech, not matchable price |
| Launch May 15 | May 10 | 9/10 | 7/10 | Speed was right; scope was wrong |

SCORING NOTES:
- Scores are consensus estimates, voted on by Priya, Nadia, and Marcus at end of meeting
- "Original score" = how good the decision seemed when we made it
- "Hindsight score" = how good the decision looks now, knowing what happened
- Hindsight scores are NOT about blame — a decision can be excellent at the time and still look worse in hindsight due to unforeseeable events

NET ASSESSMENT: We moved fast and built a good product. Our core technology (streaming, API) is genuinely better than the competition. The strategic errors were in risk assessment (underestimating competitor response) and feature scoping (binary ship/cut instead of MVP). The product fundamentals are strong — we need to fix the strategic wrapper around them.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Verdana Analytics launched on May 15 and achieved strong initial traction: 104 accounts, 16 paid, $5,510 peak MRR before repricing. API adoption was exceptional — 81% activation rate with power users building production workflows on our streaming infrastructure. Core product quality is validated.

On May 31, Rivalytics dropped pricing from $49/mo to $19/mo, funded by a $28M Series D from Sequoia. This invalidated our original pricing strategy and forced an immediate competitive response. We adjusted pricing to $24/mo monthly and $19/mo annual, and accelerated our dashboard to ship by June 18.

This document provides a complete picture of our position, the competitive landscape, and our path forward. It requires the board's input on fundraising timing.

The document is organized into six sections: launch results, competitive landscape, decision retrospective, financial position, 90-day plan, and specific asks from the board. Appendices provide supporting data tables and reference documents. Each section has been reviewed by the relevant functional lead for accuracy.

SECTION 2: COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE — WHAT CHANGED

ORIGINAL POSITIONING (May 1-30):

Our entry strategy was built on three pillars:
1. PRICE: $29/mo vs Rivalytics $49/mo (41% undercut)
2. REAL-TIME: Sub-200ms streaming vs 6-12 hour batch ETL
3. API-FIRST: Programmatic access as primary interface vs dashboard-first

This positioning was documented in the Competitive Positioning Document dated May 7 and approved by the board during the go/no-go review on May 10. The three-pillar framing was used across all sales materials, the Hacker News launch post, and Derek's outbound messaging.

STATUS OF EACH PILLAR (as of June 6):

Pillar 1 — PRICE: COLLAPSED
- Rivalytics dropped to $19/mo (61% cut from their previous $49)
- We adjusted to $24/mo monthly, $19/mo annual
- Net position: we're at parity on annual, still $5 more expensive on monthly
- The original undercutting strategy is no longer viable

Additional context on the Rivalytics pricing move: Their announcement on May 31 was accompanied by a blog post titled "Analytics for Everyone" and a press release citing the $28M Series D from Sequoia. The pricing reduction applies to their Team tier (their most popular plan). Their Enterprise tier remains at $149/mo. The $19/mo price point is almost certainly below their cost basis — Tomoko estimates their infrastructure cost per seat is approximately $8-12/mo based on publicly available information about their cloud spend and headcount, meaning they're operating on thin margins or at a loss on the Team tier to drive adoption. This is a classic land-and-expand play funded by venture capital.

Derek's sales team reported that within 48 hours of the Rivalytics announcement, three prospects who were in active trials with us forwarded the Rivalytics pricing page and asked us to match. Two of those three are now on our $19/mo annual plan. The third is still evaluating.

Pillar 2 — REAL-TIME: INTACT BUT UNDER THREAT
- Our streaming: sub-200ms latency
- Rivalytics Live: 15-minute freshness (their improvement from 6-12 hours)
- Our advantage: 4,500x faster data freshness
- Threat: Rivalytics posted 4 streaming infrastructure engineering jobs, signaling investment in real-time. With $28M, they have runway to build this over 12-18 months.
- Assessment: We have a 12-18 month window where our streaming architecture is a defensible advantage. After that, the gap narrows.

Marcus provides technical context: Rivalytics' "Live" feature appears to be a polling mechanism layered on top of their existing batch ETL pipeline — their architecture pulls from source APIs on a 15-minute cron cycle and caches the results. True sub-second streaming requires a fundamentally different architecture (event-driven, WebSocket-based, with per-connector streaming adapters). Rebuilding this is not a matter of hiring 4 engineers; it's an architectural migration that typically takes 12-18 months for a team of that size, assuming they don't have to maintain backward compatibility with their existing batch customers. The job postings specify experience with Apache Kafka, Flink, and WebSocket infrastructure, which confirms they're starting from scratch on streaming.

Marcus's confidence level on the 12-18 month window: "High. They could ship a demo of streaming for one connector in 6 months. Production-grade streaming across 40+ connectors with the reliability we already have is a different problem entirely."

Pillar 3 — API-FIRST: WEAKENED
- We still have the better API and developer experience
- But Rivalytics now has enhanced dashboards AND we don't have any dashboard yet
- Our dashboard ships June 18 (minimal version)
- Assessment: Once our dashboard ships, this pillar stabilizes. Until then, it's a liability.

Lina provides context on the dashboard timeline: The minimal dashboard spec was finalized on June 2 after the emergency strategy session. It includes a grid-based layout system with four widget types (line chart, bar chart, number card, and table), basic data binding to any API endpoint, and shareable public links. It does not include a dynamic query builder, custom date ranges beyond presets, or collaborative editing. These are scoped for Dashboard V2 in July. The team (Lina, Marcus, and one frontend contractor) is confident in the June 18 date. Lina has completed the design system and component library; Marcus has the data binding layer 60% complete as of June 5.

SECONDARY COMPETITORS:

While Rivalytics is our primary competitive concern, Derek's team has also encountered the following in sales conversations:
- Mixpanel: mentioned by 3 prospects, but focused on product analytics (not general-purpose)
- Amplitude: mentioned by 2 prospects, primarily for product/growth teams
- Metabase (open source): mentioned by 4 prospects as their current solution; they're evaluating us as a managed replacement
- Custom internal tools (Grafana + SQL + cron): mentioned by 6 prospects as their current approach

None of these represent the same direct competitive threat as Rivalytics, which occupies the exact same market position we're targeting. The Metabase and custom-tool prospects represent our best conversion opportunities — they're looking for a managed solution and are less price-sensitive because they're currently spending engineering time on maintenance.

SECTION 3: DECISION RETROSPECTIVE — WHAT WE GOT WRONG

In the interest of full transparency with the board, here is our honest assessment of our pre-launch decisions:

This retrospective was drafted collaboratively by the executive team on June 3 during a 90-minute session. Each decision is evaluated against what we knew at the time, what we should have known, and what we know now. The goal is not blame assignment but calibration improvement for future decisions.

PRICING ($29/mo): The rationale was sound at the time. We correctly identified that undercutting Rivalytics would lower switching barriers. Our error was in risk assessment — we estimated less than 5% probability that Rivalytics would drop below $29 in 12 months. This was based on their cost structure, which couldn't sustain $19. We failed to account for the possibility of external funding enabling aggressive pricing.

Tomoko's note on this: "Our competitive pricing model assumed Rivalytics was operating on revenue-funded economics. At $49/mo with their estimated 8,000+ seats, they were running at approximately $4.7M ARR with healthy margins. A rational actor wouldn't cut price by 61% from that position. What we missed is that a $28M raise changes the incentive structure — they can afford to lose money on the Team tier for 18-24 months while they build enterprise revenue. This was a fundraising-dependent scenario that we assigned insufficient probability."

Action item from the retrospective: For future pricing decisions, always model a "competitor raises a large round" scenario and stress-test pricing strategy against it, regardless of assessed probability. Owner: Tomoko. Due: standing practice.

DASHBOARD CUT: We cut the dashboard entirely to ship faster. Lina Choi (our designer) warned that users would immediately ask for it — she was right. The correct decision would have been to ship a minimal dashboard (basic charts on a grid, no dynamic query builder) which would have added approximately 2 weeks to our timeline. We framed the decision as binary (full dashboard or none) when a minimum viable option existed.

Lina's note: "I raised the dashboard concern during the feature prioritization meeting on May 4. The team's response was that our target buyer is a developer who prefers API access, and that's true — but the person who signs the purchase order is often a VP of Engineering or a business stakeholder who wants to see a screenshot of what the product looks like. An API endpoint doesn't screenshot well. The minimal dashboard I proposed at the time was roughly 80 hours of work: a grid layout, 4 widget types, data binding. We could have shipped it by May 29 instead of May 15."

Marcus's note: "In retrospect, Lina was right. I pushed back on the dashboard because I was concerned about scope creep — historically, 'minimal dashboards' expand during implementation. But the solution was to timebox the dashboard work, not eliminate it. Lesson learned."

Action item: Future feature-cut decisions must include a "minimum viable" option in addition to "full" and "none." Owner: Marcus and Lina jointly. Due: standing practice.

COMPETITIVE POSITIONING: We over-indexed on price as a differentiator. When the price pillar collapsed, our positioning was weakened. Future positioning should anchor on our defensible technical advantage (streaming architecture) rather than on pricing that can be matched.

Derek's note: "Our outbound messaging was approximately 60% price-focused and 40% real-time-focused. When Rivalytics dropped to $19, the price-focused messaging became a liability — it actually drew attention to the fact that we were now more expensive. I've already rewritten the outbound templates to lead with real-time streaming and use price as a secondary point ('competitive pricing' rather than 'cheapest option'). The new templates went live on June 4."

Action item: All external-facing materials (website, sales decks, one-pagers) to be updated to lead with real-time streaming as the primary differentiator. Price messaging to shift from "cheapest" to "fair price for premium technology." Owner: Derek, with Lina on design. Due: June 12.

SECTION 2: CI/CD IMPROVEMENTS — IN PROGRESS

I've identified four improvements to the pipeline, ranging from quick wins to longer-term investments. Prioritized by impact and effort.

IMPROVEMENT 1: PARALLEL TEST EXECUTION

Current: All 142 tests run sequentially in a single process. This is the single biggest contributor to CI time.

Proposed: Split tests into 4 parallel groups using pytest-xdist. The split is based on test file — xdist distributes test files across workers rather than individual tests, which avoids issues with test-level fixtures.

Technical details:
- Adding `pytest-xdist` to dev dependencies
- CI step changes from `uv run pytest tests/` to `uv run pytest tests/ -n 4`
- GitHub Actions runner has 4 vCPUs so 4 workers is the natural fit
- Need to verify no test interdependencies (shared DB state, file locks, etc.)
- Integration tests use separate testcontainer instances per worker — verified this works locally

Expected impact: Test step drops from 3m 22s to ~1m 10s.
Status: PR #87 open, testing on a feature branch. Amir found one test (`test_connector_sync_ordering`) that relied on test execution order — fixed by adding explicit setup in the test. Running the full suite with `-n 4` locally 10 times, all green.

IMPROVEMENT 2: DOCKER LAYER CACHING

Current: Docker builds use GitHub Actions cache, but the cache hit rate is only 60% because our requirements.txt changes frequently. Every time someone adds or bumps a dependency, the entire `pip install` layer is invalidated.

Proposed: Use a two-stage Dockerfile — base image with dependencies (cached for weeks) and app image with source code (rebuilt each time). The base image is tagged with a hash of `uv.lock` so it only rebuilds when dependencies actually change.

```
# Stage 1: Base (cached unless uv.lock changes)
FROM python:3.12-slim AS base
COPY uv.lock pyproject.toml ./
RUN uv sync --no-dev

# Stage 2: App (rebuilt each time)
FROM base AS app
COPY src/ ./src/
COPY alembic/ ./alembic/
```

The CI workflow computes `sha256(uv.lock)` and checks ECR for a base image with that tag. If it exists, Docker pulls it and only builds stage 2. If not, it builds both stages and pushes the base image.

Expected impact: Docker build drops from 1m 12s to ~25s for most builds. Full rebuilds (when dependencies change) will be ~1m 30s due to the extra push step, but these happen maybe once or twice a week.
Status: Implemented, merged in PR #85. Cache hit rate since merge: 92% (up from 60%).

IMPROVEMENT 3: MYPY INCREMENTAL MODE

Current: mypy runs a full check every CI run. As our codebase grows, this will get slower.

Proposed: Enable mypy incremental mode with persistent cache. The cache is stored as a GitHub Actions cache artifact keyed on the mypy version + Python version + commit SHA of the base branch. On PR runs, we restore the cache from the base branch and run mypy incrementally — it only rechecks files that changed.

Technical details:
- Cache key: `mypy-{mypy_version}-{python_version}-{base_branch_sha}`
- Fallback key: `mypy-{mypy_version}-{python_version}-` (latest cache from any commit)
- mypy cache directory: `.mypy_cache/` — stored and restored between runs
- Incremental mode is mypy's default, but without a persistent cache it's useless in CI (each run starts fresh)
- Risk: cache corruption could cause false negatives (mypy misses a real error). Mitigation: weekly scheduled CI run with `--no-incremental` to catch any drift.

Expected impact: Type check drops from 1m 8s to ~20s for most runs. Full check (~1m 8s) on cache miss.
Status: Testing — need to validate cache correctness. PR #89. I've been running both modes side-by-side on feature branches for the past 3 days. Incremental mode has reported the same results as full mode in all 14 runs so far.

Priya raised a concern about `--follow-imports=skip` interacting badly with incremental mode. I tested this — we don't use `--follow-imports=skip` anywhere in our mypy config, so it's not an issue. But good catch.

IMPROVEMENT 4: DEPLOY PREVIEW ENVIRONMENTS

Current: Developers test locally or on staging. No per-PR preview environments. The staging environment is shared, which means only one PR can be on staging at a time. We've had a few scheduling conflicts where Amir needed staging for a demo while Priya was testing a migration.

Proposed: Spin up an ephemeral ECS service for each PR with a unique URL (e.g., `pr-87.preview.verdana-internal.dev`). Automatically teardown when PR is closed or after 48 hours of inactivity. Each preview gets its own ECS task and connects to a shared preview RDS instance (separate from staging and production).

Architecture:
- GitHub Actions workflow triggered on PR open/sync
- Terraform module creates: ECS service (1 task), ALB target group, Route53 record
- Preview database: shared RDS instance (`preview-db`) with per-PR schema (schema name = `pr_{number}`)
- Kafka: shared preview cluster with per-PR topic prefix
- Redis: shared preview instance with per-PR key prefix
- Teardown: GitHub Actions workflow triggered on PR close, or scheduled cleanup Lambda runs every 6 hours and removes previews with no traffic for 48 hours

Expected impact: Better testing, fewer staging conflicts, ability for product/design to review before merge. Hannah (product) has been asking for this — she wants to review UI changes without cloning the repo and running locally.
Estimated cost: ~$15/PR/day (ECS + RDS connection). At our current volume (~3 open PRs at any time), that's ~$45/day or ~$1,350/month. This is significant relative to our total infra cost.
Status: Scoping phase — estimated 2 weeks of Kevin's time. Deprioritized in favor of more urgent work (DR failover testing, parallel CI). Targeting Q3 if the team decides the cost is justified.

Marcus's note: "I like the idea but want to see us hit 200 accounts before we invest in preview environments. At our current pace the staging conflicts are annoying but manageable. Revisit in July."

PROJECTED CI TIME AFTER IMPROVEMENTS (1, 2, and 3):
| Step | Current | Projected | Savings | Status |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Checkout + setup | 28s | 28s | — | No change planned |
| Install dependencies | 1m 45s | 1m 45s | — | No change planned |
| Lint (ruff) | 12s | 12s | — | Already fast |
| Type check (mypy) | 1m 8s | 20s | -48s | PR #89, testing |
| Unit tests | 3m 22s | 1m 10s | -2m 12s | PR #87, testing |
| Integration tests | 1m 35s | 1m 35s | — | No change planned |
| Build Docker image | 1m 12s | 25s | -47s | PR #85, merged |
| Push to ECR | 22s | 22s | — | Already fast |
| **Total** | **8m 42s** | **5m 57s** | **-2m 45s** | |

Target: Under 6 minutes from PR push to green CI. We're close. Once PR #87 and #89 land, I expect we'll consistently be in the 5-6 minute range. The long tail (P90) should drop from 12m 20s to under 8 minutes.

For context, the industry benchmark for similar-sized projects is 5-10 minutes. We're in good shape.

SECTION 3: AWS COST OPTIMIZATION

Two weeks of production data gave me enough signal to identify cost optimization opportunities. Below is a detailed breakdown of our AWS spend and specific findings.

CURRENT MONTHLY AWS COST BREAKDOWN:

| Service | Monthly Cost | % of Total | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| RDS (primary + replica) | $676 | 30.2% | db.r6g.xlarge × 2 |
| ElastiCache (Redis) | $1,104 | 49.3% | 6-node cluster (3 shards, 1 replica each) |
| ECS Fargate | $198 | 8.8% | 2-16 tasks, 1 vCPU / 2 GB each |
| MSK (Kafka) | $142 | 6.3% | 3-broker cluster, kafka.t3.small |
| ECR | $8 | 0.4% | Image storage |
| CloudWatch | $18 | 0.8% | Logs + metrics |
| Route53 + ALB | $52 | 2.3% | Load balancer + DNS |
| S3 | $6 | 0.3% | Backups + artifacts |
| Other (VPC, NAT, etc.) | $35 | 1.6% | NAT gateway, data transfer |
| **Total** | **$2,239** | **100%** | |

OBSERVATION: Redis is nearly half our AWS bill. This is the biggest opportunity.

FINDING 1: REDIS CLUSTER IS OVER-PROVISIONED

Current: 3-shard Redis Cluster with 1 replica per shard = 6 nodes × cache.r6g.large (13.07 GB RAM, 2 vCPU each)
Current cost: $1,104/month
Current utilization:
- Memory: 3.2% (2.5 GB used out of 78.4 GB total across cluster)
- CPU: 0.8% average, 2.1% peak
- Network: 12 Mbps average, 45 Mbps peak
- Connections: 24 average, 68 peak
- Operations: 1,200 ops/sec average, 3,800 ops/sec peak
- Cache hit rate: 94.6%

This is dramatically over-provisioned. At our current scale (112 accounts), a single cache.r6g.medium node (6.38 GB RAM) would handle our workload with room to grow to 500+ accounts. The 3-shard cluster was provisioned for a projected 10,000-account workload that we're nowhere near.

However: Marcus and I agreed pre-launch to keep the cluster to avoid a migration later. I still think that was the right call, but I want to flag the cost. Redis cluster migrations involve downtime and application changes (different connection handling for cluster vs. standalone mode).

Detailed cost comparison:

| Configuration | Nodes | Monthly Cost | Capacity | When to Use |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Current (3-shard cluster) | 6 × r6g.large | $1,104 | ~10,000 accounts | If we grow fast |
| Downsized cluster | 3 × r6g.medium | $552 | ~3,000 accounts | 200-3,000 accounts |
| Standalone + replica | 2 × r6g.medium | $368 | ~1,000 accounts | 200-1,000 accounts |
| Standalone (no replica) | 1 × r6g.medium | $184 | ~500 accounts | If we accept HA risk |

Alternative: Downgrade to a 3-node cluster with cache.r6g.medium nodes
Savings: $1,104 → $552/month = $552/month saved
Risk: If we grow faster than expected, we'd need to scale the cluster, which requires downtime. Scaling from 3 medium nodes to 6 large nodes requires a blue-green migration (~2 hours of engineering time + 15 minutes of downtime).

RECOMMENDATION: Keep current setup until we reach 200 accounts, then re-evaluate. If growth is slower than projected, downgrade in Q3. The $552/month savings is real but not urgent — it's about 25% of total infra cost.

Marcus's note: "Agreed. Flag for Q3 review. I'd rather over-pay for headroom than deal with a Redis migration while we're trying to close deals."

FINDING 2: ECS FARGATE MINIMUM TASKS

Current: Minimum 2 tasks, scaling to 16. Each task: 1 vCPU, 2 GB RAM.
Current cost: ~$198/month (based on actual usage with autoscaling)

Observation: Between midnight and 6 AM ET, traffic drops to near zero. We're paying for 2 idle tasks during those 6 hours.

Traffic pattern (requests/minute):
- 12 AM - 6 AM ET: 0-2 req/min
- 6 AM - 9 AM ET: 15-40 req/min (ramp up)
- 9 AM - 6 PM ET: 50-120 req/min (business hours)
- 6 PM - 12 AM ET: 10-30 req/min (wind down)

Alternative: Scale to 0 tasks overnight with a cold-start Lambda that wakes the service on first request. The Lambda would sit behind the ALB and, on receiving a request, trigger ECS scale-up and return a "warming up" response. Subsequent requests hit the ALB health check loop until the task is healthy.

Savings: ~$35/month (2 tasks × 6 hours/day × 30 days × Fargate pricing)
Complexity: New Lambda function, ALB listener rule changes, cold-start handling in client SDKs
Risk: Cold start latency (14 seconds) for first request after overnight. This includes ECS task provisioning (8s), container startup (4s), and application startup (2s).

RECOMMENDATION: Not worth the complexity. $35/month savings doesn't justify cold start UX impact or the engineering effort to build and maintain the scale-to-zero mechanism. If any customer's automated systems make early-morning API calls, they'd hit a 14-second delay that could cause timeouts.

Amir asked about this in standup — he agreed it's not worth it. "If even one customer has a cron job at 5 AM that times out because we wanted to save $35, that's a bad trade."

FINDING 3: CLOUDWATCH LOGS RETENTION

Current: 30-day retention on all log groups (12 log groups total)
Current cost: ~$18/month total for CloudWatch (metrics + logs)
Log volume: ~2.1 GB/day across all log groups
Observation: We're also ingesting logs into Datadog via a Firehose subscription filter. CloudWatch logs are redundant after Datadog ingestion.

Log group breakdown:

| Log Group | Daily Volume | Purpose | Datadog Ingested? |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| /ecs/verdana-api | 1.2 GB | Application logs | Yes |
| /ecs/verdana-worker | 0.5 GB | Background workers | Yes |
| /ecs/verdana-scheduler | 0.1 GB | Cron jobs | Yes |
| /rds/verdana-primary | 0.15 GB | Slow query log | Yes |
| /msk/verdana-kafka | 0.1 GB | Broker logs | No (not needed) |
| /lambda/various | 0.05 GB | Lambda invocations | Yes |
| **Total** | **~2.1 GB/day** | | |

Alternative: Reduce CloudWatch retention to 3 days (for emergency access if Datadog is down). Rely on Datadog for log analysis and alerting.
Savings: ~$5/month (not a huge number, but it's a one-line change and reduces our data surface area)
Risk: Minimal — Datadog retains logs for 15 days, which covers our needs. If Datadog has an outage lasting more than 3 days, we'd lose some log coverage, but that's an extreme edge case. We also have S3 log exports as a backup (7-day retention in the `verdana-logs-archive` bucket).

RECOMMENDATION: Implement. PR #91 changes retention to 3 days. Already tested in staging — no issues.

FINDING 4: RESERVED INSTANCES FOR RDS

Current: On-demand billing for RDS
Instance type: db.r6g.xlarge (4 vCPU, 32 GB RAM) — primary + 1 read replica
On-demand cost: $338/month per instance × 2 = $676/month total

Observation: Our database workload is consistent (primary always running, replica always running). The instance type hasn't changed since launch. CPU utilization averages 22% on the primary and 8% on the replica, with peaks at 45% and 15% respectively. We're not going to change instance types anytime soon.

Reserved Instance options:

| Option | Term | Monthly Cost (per instance) | Savings vs On-Demand | Total Monthly (2 instances) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| On-demand (current) | — | $338 | — | $676 |
| 1-year RI, no upfront | 1 year | $214 | 37% | $428 |
| 1-year RI, partial upfront | 1 year | $198 | 41% | $396 |
| 3-year RI, partial upfront | 3 years | $142 | 58% | $284 |

Alternative: 1-year Reserved Instance for both primary and replica (db.r6g.xlarge), no upfront payment
Savings: $676 → $428/month = $248/month saved (37% discount)
Commitment: 1-year term, no refund. If we need to change instance types, we'd be paying for unused RIs.

RECOMMENDATION: Wait until month 3 (August). If we're still running the same instance class, commit to the RI. We shouldn't lock into a 1-year commitment until we're confident in our scaling trajectory. If our account growth matches projections, we might need to upgrade to db.r6g.2xlarge by Q4, which would make the RI wasteful.

Marcus asked about the 3-year option. My view: too early. 3-year commitment requires high confidence in our architecture, and we're 2 weeks into production. The 58% discount is attractive but the risk of being locked into the wrong instance type outweighs the savings at this stage.

COST OPTIMIZATION SUMMARY:

| Finding | Monthly Savings | Effort | Risk | Recommendation |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Redis downsize | $552 | Medium (migration) | Medium | Defer to Q3 |
| ECS scale-to-zero | $35 | High (new infra) | Medium | Skip |
| CloudWatch retention | $5 | Low (config change) | Low | Implement now |
| RDS Reserved Instances | $248 | Low (AWS console) | Low-Medium | Defer to August |
| **Total potential** | **$840** | | | |

If we implement all four, we'd drop from $2,239/month to ~$1,399/month (37% reduction). Realistically, the CloudWatch change now and the RDS RI in August gets us to $1,986/month with minimal risk.
