SECTION 4: INFRASTRUCTURE MONITORING IMPROVEMENTS

Since launch, I've been building out our monitoring and alerting to catch issues before they become incidents. Datadog is our primary monitoring platform. Here's what's been added in the past two weeks.

NEW DASHBOARDS ADDED (post-launch):

1. "Deploy Tracker" — Shows each deployment with before/after metrics (latency, error rate, CPU). Makes it easy to identify if a deploy caused a regression. Each deploy is annotated on the time-series graphs as a vertical line with the PR number and author. The dashboard includes:
   - P50/P95/P99 latency with deploy annotations
   - Error rate (4xx and 5xx separately) with deploy annotations
   - CPU and memory utilization per ECS task
   - Active connection count
   - Deployment duration and rollback indicator
   This dashboard was invaluable during the May 19 incident — we could immediately see the latency spike correlated with the 2:15 PM deploy.

2. "Customer Health" — Per-account metrics: API call rate, error rate, connector sync status. Helps customer support identify accounts with issues before users report them. Dashboard includes:
   - Top 20 accounts by API volume (last 24h)
   - Accounts with elevated error rates (>5% 4xx or any 5xx)
   - Connector sync status grid (green/yellow/red per account per connector)
   - Account onboarding funnel (created → first API call → first connector → active)
   Hannah (product) and Lisa (customer success) both have this bookmarked. Lisa checks it every morning and has already caught two accounts with stale connectors before the customers noticed.

3. "Cost Monitor" — Daily AWS cost breakdown by service. Alerts if daily cost exceeds $120 (150% of average). This caught a Kafka log retention misconfiguration that was accumulating 5 GB/day of unnecessary logs. Details:
   - Daily cost by service (stacked bar chart)
   - 7-day rolling average cost trend
   - Cost per account (total AWS cost / active accounts) — currently $19.99/account/month
   - Projected monthly cost based on trailing 7-day average
   The Kafka log issue was interesting — MSK's default log retention is 7 days but our broker config had `log.retention.hours=-1` (infinite). We were accumulating logs that would never be cleaned up. Fixed in the MSK config on May 25, reclaimed 35 GB of storage.

4. "CI/CD Health" — Pipeline metrics pulled from GitHub Actions API. Shows:
   - CI duration trend (last 30 days)
   - Failure rate by step (tests, lint, build, etc.)
   - Deploy frequency and success rate
   - Time from PR open to deploy (lead time)
   This is a lightweight version of DORA metrics. I want to formalize this into a proper DORA dashboard eventually, but this gives us the basics.

NEW ALERTS ADDED:

| Alert | Condition | Channel | Severity | Escalation |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Deploy regression | Error rate >2× post-deploy (5-min window) | #ops-alerts | P2 | Page on-call if persists >10 min |
| Customer health | Any account 0 API calls for 24h (previously active) | #customer-health | P3 | No page — CS team reviews daily |
| Cost spike | Daily AWS cost >$120 | #ops-alerts | P3 | No page — review next business day |
| Certificate expiry | <30 days (increased from 14) | #ops-alerts | P2 | Page on-call at <7 days |
| ECS task crash | Task exits with non-zero code | #ops-alerts | P2 | Page if >2 crashes in 10 min |
| RDS connection saturation | Active connections >80% of max | #ops-alerts | P1 | Page immediately |
| Replication lag | RDS replica lag >1 second | #ops-alerts | P2 | Page if persists >5 min |
| Kafka consumer lag | Any consumer group lag >10,000 messages | #ops-alerts | P2 | Page if persists >15 min |

ALERT HISTORY (last 12 days):

| Date | Alert | Resolution |
|:---|:---|:---|
| May 17 | Cost spike ($127) | Kafka log retention issue — fixed May 25 (root cause identified later) |
| May 19 | Deploy regression | Connection pool incident — reverted deploy |
| May 23 | RDS connection saturation | Connection pool incident (P2) — resolved with per-account limits |

Four alerts in 12 days feels about right. No false positives yet, which is good — I'd rather have too few alerts than too many. Alert fatigue is a real risk and I want to keep the signal-to-noise ratio high.

SECTION 5: DATABASE PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS

Two weeks of production data reveals interesting query patterns. I pulled these metrics from our RDS Performance Insights dashboard and correlated with Datadog APM traces.

DATA VOLUME SUMMARY:
- Total rows in `events` table: 2.4 million
- Daily ingest rate: ~180,000 rows/day
- Total database size: 4.2 GB (data) + 1.8 GB (indexes) = 6.0 GB
- Table count: 34
- Largest table: `events` (4.1 GB)
- Second largest: `connector_sync_log` (180 MB)

TOP 10 SLOWEST QUERIES (by p95 latency):

| Rank | Query Pattern | P50 Latency | P95 Latency | P99 Latency | Frequency | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Aggregation with 5+ GROUP BY columns | 180ms | 340ms | 520ms | 12/day | Power users building complex reports |
| 2 | Full-text search on event payload | 140ms | 280ms | 450ms | 45/day | Users searching within JSON events |
| 3 | Cross-connector JOIN queries | 110ms | 220ms | 380ms | 8/day | Correlating data across sources |
| 4 | Time-range scan >30 days | 95ms | 180ms | 290ms | 22/day | Historical analysis |
| 5 | COUNT DISTINCT on high-cardinality column | 85ms | 165ms | 240ms | 35/day | Unique user/session counting |
| 6 | Nested JSON path query | 70ms | 140ms | 210ms | 60/day | Deep payload extraction |
| 7 | Multi-tenant account listing | 55ms | 120ms | 185ms | 200/day | Admin queries |
| 8 | Connector sync status check | 40ms | 85ms | 130ms | 500/day | Frequent polling |
| 9 | Event insert (batch 1000) | 35ms | 75ms | 110ms | 300/day | Kafka consumer writes |
| 10 | Simple SELECT by ID | 1ms | 3ms | 8ms | 5,000/day | API key validation, etc. |

ANALYSIS:
- None of these are concerning at current scale. The slowest query (340ms p95) is well within acceptable limits. Our API SLA target is 500ms p95, and even the heaviest aggregation query is under that.
- The connector sync status check (#8) is called frequently and could benefit from Redis caching. Filed as a ticket (JIRA-247). The sync status only changes when a sync completes (every 15 minutes), so caching with a 60-second TTL would eliminate ~95% of these queries. Amir is picking this up for his June sprint.
- Event batch inserts (#9) are efficiently using COPY command. No optimization needed. We tested INSERT vs COPY during pre-launch and COPY was 4x faster for batch sizes >500.
- Full-text search (#2) will become a problem at scale. The current implementation uses PostgreSQL's built-in `tsvector` with a GIN index, which works fine for 2.4M rows but will degrade at 50M+. We should evaluate pgvector or Elasticsearch for search workloads in Q3. I've created a research ticket (JIRA-248) for Priya to investigate options.
- The cross-connector JOIN (#3) is the most architecturally interesting query. It joins the `events` table with itself across different `connector_id` values within the same account. At scale, this will need a materialized view or pre-computed correlation table. Not urgent at 8 queries/day.

INDEX USAGE REPORT:

| Index | Table | Size | Scans/Day | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| events_pkey | events | 52 MB | 5,000 | Primary key — heavily used |
| idx_events_account_created | events | 180 MB | 4,200 | Account + timestamp range — our workhorse index |
| idx_events_connector_id | events | 95 MB | 800 | Connector-specific queries |
| idx_events_payload_tsvector | events | 320 MB | 45 | Full-text search — large index, low usage |
| idx_events_event_type | events | 68 MB | 350 | Event type filtering |
| idx_connector_sync_account | connector_sync_log | 12 MB | 500 | Sync status lookups |

The full-text index (`idx_events_payload_tsvector`) is our largest index at 320 MB but only gets 45 scans/day. This is fine for now — the index is what makes those full-text queries possible at all. Without it, those 280ms queries would be multi-second sequential scans.

No unused indexes detected. All indexes have been scanned at least once in the past 7 days.

CONNECTION POOL UTILIZATION:
- Pool size: 100 connections (configured in pgbouncer)
- Average active: 18 of 100 connections (18%)
- Peak active: 52 of 100 connections (52%) — during the May 23 incident
- After per-account limits: peak 38 of 100 (38%)
- Idle connections: typically 4-6 (pgbouncer keeps a small warm pool)
- Connection wait time (p95): <1ms (no queuing)
- Assessment: healthy headroom. At current growth rate, we'd hit 80% utilization (our alert threshold) at approximately 600 accounts. We'll need to increase the pool size or optimize connection usage before then.

REPLICATION LAG:
- Average: 12ms
- P95: 45ms
- Peak: 180ms (during bulk data ingestion from a new customer onboarding with 500K historical events)
- Time above 100ms: 3 minutes total in 12 days (all during that one bulk import)
- Assessment: well within acceptable limits for read-after-write consistency on the replica. Our application uses the replica for read-heavy dashboard queries and the primary for writes and real-time reads. The 12ms average lag means dashboard data is effectively real-time.

VACUUM AND MAINTENANCE:
- autovacuum running normally on all tables
- No bloat detected (checked with `pgstattuple`)
- `events` table: 0.3% dead tuple ratio (healthy — autovacuum is keeping up with the insert rate)
- Last manual ANALYZE: not needed, autovacuum handles it
- WAL disk usage: 2.1 GB (well within the 50 GB allocated)

```
└──────────┘  └──────────┘  └─────┘  └────────┘
```
